turns.zh: 是啊，没错。我是说，最近有一个事件，很多人联系我，他们说，哦特朗普说他要处决利兹·切尼。我说，这完全是……他根本不是那个意思。他说的只是，如果利兹·切尼真的要在前线作战，她应该三思而后行。没错，因为如果你不必冒死在战壕里的风险，发动战争很容易，当好战分子很容易。如果人们在华盛顿特区享用豪华晚餐，而人们在战壕里被屠杀，你感受不到痛苦，你不承担风险，死的是别人。这很残忍，缺乏同理心。而特朗普说的只是，利兹·切尼会不那么好战，因为她和她父亲一样是个大战贩子，如果她真的必须亲自上前线作战的话。与此同时，他们说他应该被枪毙，这完全是谎言。但周末有无数人打电话给我说，哦特朗普说他要让利兹·切尼面对行刑队。我说，这太离谱了，是个谎言。而传统媒体大肆传播这个谎言。

turns.en: Yeah, it's crazy. It's just wild to see. And if it wasn't for Twitter or X now, I don't think we would know about all this stuff. I think it would be very difficult. For you, I think YouTube throttled, they did something weird, they won't say what they did, but they did something weird with the Trump interview that I did, yeah, where you couldn't find it. It doesn't make sense, like made no sense. I mean, it's like the biggest interview on Earth, yeah, and you can't find it. Not only that, it wasn't trending. It wasn't trending. You're like, this just no excuse for that man, no excuse.
turns.zh: 是啊，太疯狂了。看着真是不可思议。如果没有 Twitter 或现在的 X，我想我们不会知道所有这些事情。我认为会非常困难。对你来说，我认为 YouTube 限制了流量，他们做了一些奇怪的事，他们不会说他们做了什么，但他们对我做的特朗普采访做了奇怪的事，你找不到它。这说不通，完全说不通。我是说，这是地球上最大的采访，而你找不到它。不仅如此，它没有上趋势。没有上趋势。你会想，这完全没有借口，老兄，没有借口。

turns.en: There's no excuse. It was getting a million views, 1.4 an hour, 1.5 an hour, yeah, and it wasn't trending. And it's like your channel is a known channel, it's not like it was started yesterday. It's like this is a high trust channel, you're not trying to sell scam crypto coins or something. So well, thank God we put it on X as well, because I think just with your account and my account alone, it's like 70 million views. Yeah, exactly.
turns.zh: 没有借口。它每小时有 100 万、140 万、150 万的观看量，却没有上趋势。而且你的频道是知名频道，不是昨天才开始的。这是一个高信任度的频道，你不是在推销骗人的加密货币什么的。所以，谢天谢地我们也把它放到了 X 上，因为仅凭你的账号和我的账号，就有大约 7000 万观看量。是啊，没错。

turns.en: Yeah, well, it's like you can't hide things anymore because of you. And if it wasn't for you, I think they would have had total control of social media by now. They would have banned so many accounts during the pandemic, so many dissenting scientists and doctors and physicians, so many conspiracy theorists, so many people that colored outside the lines. They would have done that everywhere. And it probably would have... I think even at what's going on at Facebook, they're being more lenient. You hear Zuckerberg talking about taking a more libertarian stance, that's entirely reaction to the way Twitter has kind of moved the watermark. Exactly.
turns.zh: 是啊，嗯，就像因为你们，事情再也藏不住了。如果不是你，我想他们现在早就完全控制了社交媒体。疫情期间他们会封禁那么多账号，那么多持不同意见的科学家、医生、医师，那么多阴谋论者，那么多越界的人。他们会在所有地方都这么做。而且很可能已经……我认为即使在 Facebook 上，他们也在变得更宽容。你听到扎克伯格谈论采取更自由意志主义的立场，这完全是对 Twitter 移动水印方式的反应。没错。

turns.en: So as soon as any company steps out of line and is willing to actually have the truth debated on their platform, it forces the other platforms to allow things to be more truthful, to not censor, because their censorship becomes glaringly obvious. Yes, um, and you know the best thing I found for as a rebuttal, like if somebody if there's a hoax, is just go to the source material. You know, if you think somebody thinks, oh you know Trump said that we should put Liz Cheney in a firing squad, I'm like let me send you a link to X so you can watch his video. That's the best way. Don't take my opinion for it, don't take anyone's opinion for it, go to the source material. And Community Notes, yes.
turns.zh: 所以一旦任何公司越界，愿意让真相在他们的平台上被辩论，就会迫使其他平台允许更多真相，不去审查，因为他们的审查变得非常明显。是的，嗯，你知道我发现最好的反驳方法，比如如果有人有骗局，就是直接去看原始材料。你知道，如果你认为有人觉得，哦特朗普说我们应该把利兹·切尼送上行刑队，我会说让我给你发一个 X 的链接，这样你可以看他的视频。这是最好的方法。不要相信我的观点，不要相信任何人的观点，去看原始材料。还有社区注释，是的。

turns.en: And Community Notes is the best, it's awesome, it's incredible because everybody gets checked, yes, including me. And with Community Notes, all the software is open source and all the data is open source, so you can recreate any given note independently. That's amazing, yeah, that's how it's total absolute transparency in every way. You know, sometimes I get asked, oh can you remove a note, you know mostly by the left but sometimes by the right. I'm like, I don't even remove notes on my own account, nothing. And by the way, everything is totally open, so if I did that, it would stick out like a sore thumb immediately, like it's not going to be subtle.
turns.zh: 社区注释是最好的，它很棒，不可思议，因为每个人都会被检查，是的，包括我。而且社区注释的所有软件都是开源的，所有数据也是开源的，所以你可以独立重现任何一条注释。这太棒了，是的，这就是它在各方面完全透明的方式。你知道，有时有人问我，哦你能删除一条注释吗，主要是左派，但有时是右派。我说，我连自己账号上的注释都不删，什么都不删。而且顺便说一句，一切都是完全开放的，所以如果我那么做了，会立刻像拇指一样突出，不会很微妙。

turns.en: Is the best counter to misinformation?
turns.zh: 对抗错误信息的最佳方式是什么？

turns.en: Yes, absolutely. Let everybody look at it and say, okay, here's what the actual facts say. Yes, exactly. The counter to misinformation is better information. Not just that, but having it checked in real time by the community, so you have millions of people that can go over it and debate whether or not this is true or that's true. And, like I said, the best way to understand the truth of things is don't take anyone's opinion for it; look at the source material. Look at what someone actually said, look at what someone actually did, look at the real videos of the situation, and then you can actually know what's real.
turns.zh: 是的，绝对如此。让每个人都能看到它，然后说，好吧，这是实际的事实。没错。对抗错误信息的最佳方式就是提供更好的信息。不仅如此，还要让社区实时核查，这样就有数百万人可以仔细检查并辩论这是真的还是假的。而且，就像我说的，理解真相的最佳方式不是听信任何人的观点，而是查看原始材料。看看某人实际说了什么，实际做了什么，看看真实情况的视频，然后你才能真正知道什么是真实的。

turns.en: As of today, when you were literally on your way here, you sent me this text saying that they're trying to lock you up in jail, Pennsylvania. Tell me what is happening.
turns.zh: 就在今天，你来的路上，你给我发了这条短信，说他们想把你关进监狱，宾夕法尼亚州。告诉我发生了什么。

turns.en: Well, you know, there's the classic sort of Soros DA situation. So we're making a lot of progress in Pennsylvania. I've given a whole bunch of talks throughout the state because Pennsylvania is the lynchpin in this election. Whoever wins Pennsylvania wins the election. I spent three years in Pennsylvania; I went to college in Philadelphia. So it's not like I'm a total stranger to the state. We organized this petition in support of the Constitution, which I think is a good thing, and specifically asking people to—we wanted this to be like registered voters in swing states. Basically, we want to send a message to the politicians to say that the people care about the Constitution because there have been all these attacks on the Constitution. Especially on the Democrat side, they've been repeatedly saying that the First Amendment is an obstacle because they claim it enables disinformation and misinformation. I'm like, yo, there's a reason for the First Amendment. Freedom of speech—the reason the founders of the country put freedom of speech there is because they came from countries where if you spoke your mind, you would get shot or imprisoned. That's why the First Amendment exists. And the Second Amendment is there to stop the tyranny of government. The Second Amendment, the right to bear arms, is there to protect freedom of speech. I've had these debates especially with people in LA because they want to take everyone's guns away. I'm like, can you guarantee me that we will never have a tyrannical government in the United States? Can you make that guarantee? They're like, well, nobody can make that guarantee. I'm like, then we need to keep our guns because that's what's going to stop it. That sounds crazy for people to hear because they think about gun violence and gun problems, but that's the reality of the world we live in: tyranny is possible, and it exists other places, and it's slowly rearing its head in the UK. I think the number of people that have been arrested for just social media posts is bananas. It's in the thousands. Several thousand people have been given prison sentences in the UK for social media posts where there was no explicit link to actual violence, but they just said it encouraged violence. Well, did anyone actually do anything as a result of that media post? Well, no. And then they have a prison overcrowding situation in the UK, so they are quite literally releasing convicted pedophiles and putting people in jail for Facebook posts. That's an actual thing happening in Britain. That is so wild. What's going on is insane.
turns.zh: 嗯，你知道，典型的索罗斯式地方检察官情况。我们在宾夕法尼亚州取得了很大进展。我在全州做了很多演讲，因为宾夕法尼亚州是这次选举的关键。谁赢得宾夕法尼亚州，谁就赢得大选。我在宾夕法尼亚州待了三年，我在费城上的大学。所以我对这个州并不陌生。我们组织了这个支持宪法的请愿，我认为这是一件好事，具体是要求人们——我们希望这能针对摇摆州的注册选民。基本上，我们想向政客们传递一个信息：人民关心宪法，因为宪法一直受到攻击。尤其是在民主党方面，他们一再声称第一修正案是一个障碍，因为他们说它助长了虚假信息和错误信息。我就想，嘿，第一修正案是有原因的。言论自由——开国元勋之所以把言论自由写进去，是因为他们来自那些如果你说出自己的想法就会被枪杀或监禁的国家。这就是第一修正案存在的原因。而第二修正案是为了阻止政府的暴政。第二修正案，即持有武器的权利，是为了保护言论自由。我特别和洛杉矶的人辩论过，因为他们想夺走所有人的枪。我说，你能保证我们永远不会在美国出现暴政政府吗？你能做出这个保证吗？他们说，嗯，没人能做出这个保证。我说，那我们就得保留枪支，因为那是阻止暴政的手段。人们听到这个会觉得疯狂，因为他们想到的是枪支暴力和枪支问题，但这就是我们生活的世界的现实：暴政是可能的，它在其他地方存在，而且正在英国慢慢抬头。我认为仅仅因为社交媒体帖子而被捕的人数已经多得离谱了，有数千人。在英国，有几千人因为社交媒体帖子被判刑，而这些帖子与实际的暴力行为没有明确联系，只是被说成鼓励暴力。那么，有没有人因为那个帖子真的做了什么？没有。然后英国监狱人满为患，所以他们实际上是在释放已定罪的恋童癖者，却把发 Facebook 帖子的人关进监狱。这是英国真实发生的事情。太疯狂了。这一切简直荒谬。

turns.en: And what's insane to me is that it's all being encouraged by the left. Ketanji Brown Jackson, John Kerry—John Kerry was one of the people who said, on camera a few weeks ago, that the First Amendment is an obstacle to fighting misinformation. That's crazy. That's such a crazy thing to say when you have a solution in Community Notes. You have a solution in something like that that could clear everything up, any confusion within a day or two. And even without a Community Note, you can reply to a post with evidence that shows that the post is wrong. You don't even need Community Notes. I mean, Community Notes is helpful because it sticks to the original note, but in the replies you can say, here's why you're wrong, here are the reasons and here's the evidence. The argument is that people are too unsophisticated, that they're not going to research these things, they're going to be a victim of misinformation, so they're going to read something incorrect, they're going to run with it, people are going to die, we're going to ruin the world because people believed in misinformation. It's a stupid argument because it's an argument that they're too dumb to know what's right or wrong. If you know it's misinformation, why do you think that you're smarter than everybody who reads that? Exactly. And obviously anyone on the X system knows that things are posted and then there are replies and there are rebuttals and it's immediately corrected. But where are the corrections for the legacy media? When broadcast media say false things all the time, but it's a one-way street; there's no rebuttal, there's no counter. Who has apologized for being incorrect? Did Rachel Maddow ever apologize for telling everybody that if you get the COVID vaccine you're never going to get COVID, it won't, the virus stops with you? Never. No one ever. It was not true at the time, there was no evidence to support it at the time, it's pure propaganda, and she said it. The Russia gate hoax—for three years they said that he was Putin's toy and that Putin had him compromised. The Steele dossier was completely fabricated by a lawyer, Perkins Coie, who was paid by the Clinton campaign. Literally crazy. And still people think the Russia hoax is real. And there's no repercussions, no one had to apologize. Hillary could never come out and apologize for that, and people still listen to her. The whole thing is crazy, and it's all coming from the left. Growing up as a person who was on the left my whole life, it doesn't make any sense.
turns.zh: 让我觉得疯狂的是，这一切都是左派在鼓励。凯坦吉·布朗·杰克逊、约翰·克里——约翰·克里几周前在镜头前说，第一修正案是打击错误信息的障碍。这太疯狂了。当你有社区笔记这样的解决方案时，说出这种话简直太离谱了。社区笔记可以在一天或两天内澄清所有困惑。即使没有社区笔记，你也可以回复帖子，用证据证明帖子是错的。你甚至不需要社区笔记。我的意思是，社区笔记很有用，因为它会附在原帖上，但在回复中你可以说，为什么你错了，理由是什么，证据是什么。他们的论点是人们太不成熟了，不会去研究这些东西，会成为错误信息的受害者，所以他们读到错误的东西就会信以为真，然后人们会死，世界会毁灭，因为人们相信了错误信息。这是个愚蠢的论点，因为它等于说他们太笨了，分不清对错。如果你知道那是错误信息，为什么你认为你比所有读到它的人更聪明？没错。显然，X 系统上的任何人都知道，帖子发布后会有回复、有反驳，并且会立即得到纠正。但传统媒体的纠正在哪里？广播媒体经常说假话，但那是单向的；没有反驳，没有反击。有谁因为错误而道歉过吗？雷切尔·玛多有没有为告诉每个人如果你接种了新冠疫苗就永远不会得新冠、病毒在你这里就停止了而道歉？从来没有。没有人道过歉。当时那不是真的，当时没有证据支持，那是纯粹的宣传，她说了。通俄门骗局——三年来他们说他（特朗普）是普京的玩物，普京控制了他。斯蒂尔档案完全是 Perkins Coie 律所的一名律师捏造的，而该律所是由克林顿竞选团队付钱的。简直疯了。仍然有人认为通俄门骗局是真的。而且没有任何后果，没有人需要道歉。希拉里从未出来为此道歉，人们仍然听她的。整件事太疯狂了，而且都来自左派。作为一个从小就是左派的人，这完全说不通。

turns.en: Same. I mean, I was on the left until like three years ago. It's not the left anymore. It's just like, I think we obviously want freedom. We want freedom.
turns.zh: 一样。我是说，直到大约三年前我还是左派。但这不再是左派了。我觉得我们显然想要自由。我们想要自由。

turns.en: Maximize personal liberty. I think we want to be kind to people, we want to have empathy, but it's very important to have personal freedom and a merit-based society. The left wants to oppress your freedoms, especially freedom of speech, and they want a non-merit-based society with race-based and sex-based preferences. But we just want people to succeed based on their skills and hard work. If they don't want people to express themselves about particular issues, then they're not doing the will of the people. If they're trying to suppress people's ability to communicate, they're only doing that because they want to do things that people don't want them to do and they want to silence opposition. That's all it is. The fact that people can't see that—they want to call Trump a fascist—the whole thing is through the looking glass. It's one hoax after another that they're perpetrating against Trump. They try to call the rally at Madison Square Gardens a Nazi rally. I'm like, there was literally an Israeli flag in the audience. I think about a quarter of the speakers were Jewish. There were people of every race, color, creed, religion at that rally. Tell me what about that is Nazi. No. And yet it was portrayed as a Nazi rally. MSNBC literally showed video of the Nazi rally from the 1930s and compared it to the Trump rally, ignoring the fact that Jimmy Carter spoke there. There have been dozens of political rallies at Madison Square Gardens—dozens on the Democrat side. People on X show: here's Jimmy Carter, here's Bill Clinton. Actually, it looks like every presidential candidate on the Democrat side has done a rally at Madison Square Gardens. Are they Nazis too? But what they're doing is preying on low-information voters who aren't engaged actively on social media, who don't have the time to look through everything.
turns.zh: 最大化个人自由。我认为我们想要善待他人，要有同理心，但个人自由和基于功绩的社会非常重要。左派想要压制你的自由，尤其是言论自由，他们想要一个非功绩的社会，基于种族和性别的偏好。但我们只希望人们凭借技能和努力获得成功。如果他们不让人们就特定问题表达自己，那他们就没有顺应民意。如果他们试图压制人们的沟通能力，那只是因为他们想做人们不希望他们做的事，并想压制反对声音。仅此而已。人们看不到这一点——他们想称特朗普为法西斯——整件事就像透过镜子看世界。他们针对特朗普的骗局一个接一个。他们试图把麦迪逊广场花园的集会称为纳粹集会。我说，观众席里明明有以色列国旗。大约四分之一的演讲者是犹太人。集会上有各种种族、肤色、信仰和宗教的人。告诉我那哪里像纳粹？不。然而它被描绘成纳粹集会。MSNBC 甚至播放了 1930 年代纳粹集会的视频，并将其与特朗普集会对比，忽略了吉米·卡特也曾在那里演讲。麦迪逊广场花园举办过几十场政治集会——民主党方面就有几十场。X 上的人展示：这是吉米·卡特，这是比尔·克林顿。实际上，每位民主党总统候选人都在麦迪逊广场花园举办过集会。他们也是纳粹吗？但他们是在利用那些不积极参与社交媒体、没有时间仔细核查一切的低信息选民。

turns.en: Exactly. People who only look at legacy mainstream media have a totally different worldview than if they're on X, seeing the actual flow of argument and the actual evidence. What was the pushback when you released the Twitter Files? I think the Twitter Files is probably one of the most important things in this age of information for understanding the influence that government has on social media and discourse. When we found out that the government was actually asking Twitter to remove factual posts—did they do the same to Facebook? They had them throttle pieces of one of Tucker Carlson's shows, suppress the views by 50% of factual information.
turns.zh: 没错。只看传统主流媒体的人，与在 X 上看到真实论证和证据的人，世界观完全不同。当你发布 Twitter 文件时，遇到了什么阻力？我认为 Twitter 文件是信息时代理解政府对社交媒体和话语影响力的最重要事件之一。当我们发现政府实际上要求 Twitter 删除事实性帖子时——他们对 Facebook 也做了同样的事吗？他们让 Facebook 限制塔克·卡尔森节目的片段，将事实信息的浏览量压制了 50%。

turns.en: There was massive government interference in Twitter, but Twitter welcomed it. Old Twitter was controlled by far-left activists. They welcomed the government interference. They got paid by the government for it—millions of dollars for suppressing information. A bunch of it was flat out illegal. The FBI had this magic portal into the Twitter system, but all communication in that portal was auto-deleted after two weeks, which breaks federal FOIA laws. So we don't even know what was said because it was all deleted after two weeks.
turns.zh: 政府对 Twitter 进行了大规模干预，但 Twitter 对此表示欢迎。旧 Twitter 由极左活动人士控制。他们欢迎政府干预，并因此从政府那里获得报酬——数百万美元用于压制信息。其中很多行为完全违法。FBI 有一个进入 Twitter 系统的神奇门户，但该门户中的所有通信在两周后自动删除，这违反了联邦信息自由法。所以我们甚至不知道说了什么，因为两周后全被删了。

turns.en: That's insane. It's so crazy that people thought that was okay. It's not okay. It's unconstitutional. No one would want the government to have that kind of access. What was the blowback when all that stuff got released? You had to anticipate problems when you released that. What happened?
turns.zh: 这太疯狂了。人们竟然认为这没问题，这太离谱了。这不对，违宪。没人希望政府拥有那种访问权限。当所有这些被曝光时，反弹如何？你发布时肯定预料到会有问题。发生了什么？

turns.en: We lost a lot of advertising dollars. Which is crazy because it's essentially one of the most important forms of journalism: exposing government corruption. The weird thing is, the left used to be big on exposing government corruption, but once they control the government, they no longer want to expose it. They want to pretend the left-wing government is incapable of corruption because we're on the good side.
turns.zh: 我们损失了大量广告收入。这很疯狂，因为这本质上是新闻业最重要的形式之一：揭露政府腐败。奇怪的是，左派过去非常热衷于揭露政府腐败，但一旦他们控制了政府，就不再想揭露了。他们想假装左翼政府不可能腐败，因为我们站在正义的一边。

turns.en: I think it's just that whoever is in power doesn't want the other side to hurt them. As you pointed out, the left historically, up until maybe 10 years ago, was the free speech party, and now it's the anti-free speech party. They use words like 'hate speech' and 'misinformation' and 'disinformation,' but these are propaganda words. Who's defining hate speech? Who's defining misinformation? The government? Do you really trust the government to make that definition? The whole point of the First Amendment is that you do not trust the government. Especially when they're wrong and there are no repercussions. Like with the lab leak theory—if you even presented the argument that maybe the coronavirus lab where they were working on the exact same virus that got released, hey maybe that's where it came from, since that's where the virus started, they'd kick you right off YouTube.
turns.zh: 我认为只是当权者不想让对手伤害自己。正如你指出的，左派历史上直到大约 10 年前还是言论自由党，现在却成了反言论自由党。他们使用“仇恨言论”、“错误信息”、“虚假信息”等词汇，但这些是宣传用语。谁来定义仇恨言论？谁来定义错误信息？政府？你真的信任政府来做这个定义吗？第一修正案的全部意义就在于不信任政府。尤其是当他们错了且没有后果时。比如实验室泄漏理论——如果你提出论点，也许那个研究完全相同病毒的冠状病毒实验室，嘿，也许病毒就是从那里来的，因为病毒起源于那里，他们就会把你踢出 YouTube。

turns.en: Yes, exactly. It's like, do you think maybe it could have come from the place called the Novel Coronavirus Research Institute? That John Stewart bit he did on COVID was amazing. 'What does it say on the door again? Can I see your business card?' And COVID resisted it with every fiber of his being. John Stewart got off his chair and started walking around to try to take control. Good on John. Then the left tried to cancel John Stewart, of course. Meanwhile, he was right. No apologies. And the whole Fauci thing—any criticism of Fauci is like an effing demon. If you read RFK's book 'The Real Anthony Fauci,' if the facts are in there, it's true. It's all referenced; you can find the sources. On top of it, he's never been sued for that book, which doesn't make any sense if he just made up a bunch of lies. He would get sued. So the guy's a monster. I think so.
turns.zh: 是的，没错。就像，你觉得它可能来自那个叫“新型冠状病毒研究所”的地方吗？约翰·斯图尔特那段关于新冠的表演太棒了。“门上写着什么？我能看看你的名片吗？”新冠拼命抵抗。约翰·斯图尔特从椅子上站起来，开始走动以掌控局面。干得好，约翰。然后左派当然试图封杀约翰·斯图尔特。而他是对的。没有道歉。还有整个福奇事件——任何对福奇的批评都像见了鬼一样。如果你读了 RFK 的书《真正的安东尼·福奇》，如果事实都在里面，那就是真的。全部有引用，你可以找到来源。而且，他从未因那本书被起诉，如果他编造了一堆谎言，那根本说不通。他会被起诉的。所以那家伙是个怪物。我也这么认为。

turns.en: Yeah, I think so too. Just looking at the lies that he told...
turns.zh: 是的，我也这么认为。看看他撒的那些谎……

turns.en: The way he tried to define gain-of-function research to Rand Paul, but I think a lot of people out there don't realize Fauci funded the bioweapons research that was going on in Wuhan. He bank-shotted it off EcoHealth, a fake nonprofit in the US, and they sent it to Wuhan. Obama put the skids on that; he stopped it in 2014.
turns.zh: 他试图向兰德·保罗定义功能获得研究的方式，但我觉得很多人不知道福奇资助了武汉的生物武器研究。他通过美国一个虚假的非营利组织 EcoHealth 间接转钱，然后他们把钱送到了武汉。奥巴马在 2014 年叫停了这件事。

turns.en: Yes, I mean, to give Obama some credit, he actually looked at this and said, 'Hey, this is crazy,' and he did stop the so-called gain-of-function—again, a propaganda word. Because what is the function they're talking about? Death, right? So if you use the right word, this is 'death maximization.' Then you're like, 'Oh, hey guys, should we fund bioweapon research into death maximization?' Because that's what gain-of-function means. It means making a disease so that people can get it and give it to people. And by the way, what's that function again? Oh, the function is death. So just call it a death-maximizing virus if you're doing research on that. And the idea behind this research is so that we can cure these things. How come you don't have a cure? Start with a cure first, disease second. It doesn't make any sense. You guys had no strategy for dealing with it if it got out. So you had to make up this new vaccine in record time, Operation Warp Speed, released to the people with very little testing. It's crazy. The whole thing's crazy. And everybody just went along with it. Looney Tunes next level.
turns.zh: 是的，我的意思是，给奥巴马一些肯定，他确实看到了这一点，说“嘿，这太疯狂了”，他确实停止了所谓的功能获得——再说一次，这是一个宣传词汇。因为他们说的功能是什么？死亡，对吧？所以如果你用正确的词，这就是“死亡最大化”。然后你会想，“哦，嘿，伙计们，我们应该资助死亡最大化的生物武器研究吗？”因为这就是功能获得的意思。它意味着制造一种疾病，让人们感染并传播给他人。顺便问一下，那个功能又是什么？哦，功能就是死亡。所以如果你在做这方面的研究，就干脆叫它死亡最大化病毒。这项研究背后的想法是我们可以治愈这些东西。为什么你没有治愈方法？先有治愈方法，再有疾病。这完全说不通。你们没有应对它泄露的策略。所以你们不得不以创纪录的速度制造这种新疫苗，即“曲速行动”，在几乎没有测试的情况下就投放给人们。这太疯狂了。整件事都疯了。而且每个人都跟着走。简直是卡通片级别的疯狂。

turns.en: It was fascinating to watch people step in line. That's like one of the biggest scams of all time, and everybody got in line. When you take it back to when pharmaceutical drug companies were able to advertise on media in the 1990s, that changed everything. Because we don't have socialized medicine, it's a complete profit scam. They went hard claiming all sorts of things that were never researched, not supported by data—like the fact that it would stop transmission, stop infection, that it was safe for pregnant women, safe for children. All of it. And they pushed it on the whole world. If you didn't say that at a cocktail party, you were a pariah, an antivaxer. It was totally psycho. It was like being a Holocaust denier; you get kicked out of polite society. Exactly bananas.
turns.zh: 看着人们排队跟进真是令人着迷。这是有史以来最大的骗局之一，每个人都排着队。当你回溯到 20 世纪 90 年代制药公司能够在媒体上做广告时，一切都变了。因为我们没有公费医疗，这完全是一个利润骗局。他们大肆宣称各种从未研究过、没有数据支持的事情——比如它能阻止传播、阻止感染、对孕妇安全、对儿童安全。所有这些都是。他们把它推向全世界。如果你在鸡尾酒会上不这么说，你就是贱民，是反疫苗者。这完全疯了。就像否认大屠杀一样；你会被上流社会踢出去。真是疯了。

turns.en: I should say I'm generally pro-vaccine overall. I think we should look at these things, but I believe in the scientific method. So you shouldn't blanket-accept that any given medication or treatment is 100% good. You should always view it with some skepticism, especially when you're getting data from pharmaceutical drug companies that have a long history of criminal conduct. They have a vested interest in the research. It's like asking tobacco companies whether smoking is dangerous. According to our scientists, everything's fine. They lied in court forever. The same thing they did with OxyContin when they said it wasn't addictive. They have a long history of being full of it if it makes them money. That's what they do. They've literally lost multi-billion-dollar lawsuits.
turns.zh: 我应该说，我总体上支持疫苗。我认为我们应该审视这些东西，但我相信科学方法。所以你不应该全盘接受任何药物或治疗是 100% 好的。你应该总是带着一些怀疑来看待，尤其是当数据来自有着长期犯罪历史的制药公司时。他们在研究中有利可图。这就像问烟草公司吸烟是否危险一样。根据我们的科学家的说法，一切都很好。他们在法庭上一直撒谎。他们对待奥施康定也是如此，当时他们说它不会上瘾。他们有着悠久的历史，只要有钱赚就满口谎言。这就是他们做的。他们确实输掉了数十亿美元的诉讼。

ts.sec: Vioxx scandal and FDA approval issues

turns.en: You have amazing scientists, clinical researchers who develop incredible drugs. That's their job—to figure out new ways to cure things. Then you have the money people. The problem is when you assume they're only doing it to help people, but they have this other faction that are just numbers people, all they care about is maximizing profits. They have an obligation to their shareholders to make the most money possible, so they just want to push it on everybody. Like the Vioxx scandal: there were internal emails showing they knew there would be cardiovascular events, people would get strokes, and they said, 'I think we're still going to do well.' They made like $12 billion, got fined $7 billion, and 50,000 to 60,000 people died. One of them was a friend of mine. He got a stroke and died—no, he lived, but he was a really healthy guy, and after that he had neurological problems. He took Vioxx, and all of a sudden he was slurring his words, couldn't concentrate. People thought he was having a stroke, took him to the hospital. Then there was a giant class-action lawsuit, Vioxx got pulled from the market. The whole thing's crazy. There's a long history of this. I think about one-third of the drugs the FDA approves gets pulled. That's bananas. You're saying one-third of the things you say are okay, but yet you're trying to stop MDMA therapy for veterans.
turns.zh: 你有出色的科学家、临床研究人员，他们开发了不可思议的药物。那是他们的工作——找出治愈疾病的新方法。然后你有那些搞钱的人。问题在于，当你以为他们只是为了帮助别人时，他们还有另一派，只是数字人，他们只关心利润最大化。他们对股东有义务，要尽可能多地赚钱，所以他们只想把药推给所有人。比如万络丑闻：内部邮件显示他们知道会有心血管事件，人们会中风，但他们说“我认为我们仍然会做得很好”。他们赚了大约 120 亿美元，被罚款 70 亿美元，5 万到 6 万人死亡。其中一个是我的朋友。他中风了，死了——不，他活了下来，但他本来是个非常健康的人，之后就有了神经问题。他服用了万络，突然之间他说话含糊不清，无法集中注意力。人们以为他中风了，把他送到医院。然后有了一起大规模集体诉讼，万络被撤出市场。整件事都疯了。这种事情有很长的历史。我认为 FDA 批准的药物中大约三分之一后来被撤回了。这太疯狂了。你说你认可的东西中有三分之一有问题，但你却试图阻止 MDMA 疗法用于退伍军人。

turns.en: They should let MDMA through honestly. I think that would actually help a lot of people. It would help a lot of people. There are a lot of different therapies, specifically psilocybin, ibogaine. The fact that you have to go to Mexico to get ibogaine therapy for veterans—so many guys I've talked to have gone over there, and it's completely given them a clean slate, refreshed their mind, a totally new perspective on life, alleviated depression, cured addictions. Illegal. Yeah, illegal. OxyContin, go get it.
turns.zh: 说实话，他们应该让 MDMA 通过。我认为这确实能帮助很多人。它能帮助很多人。有很多不同的疗法，特别是裸盖菇素、伊博格碱。事实上，你必须去墨西哥才能为退伍军人获得伊博格碱疗法——我谈过的很多人都去过那里，这完全给了他们一个全新的开始，刷新了他们的思想，对生活有了全新的视角，缓解了抑郁，治愈了成瘾。但这是非法的。是的，非法的。奥施康定，去拿吧。

turns.en: I know some people whose life was ruined by OxyContin.
turns.zh: 我认识一些人，他们的生活被奥施康定毁了。

turns.en: Yeah, because it really depends on individual biochemistry. To me, opioids are not addictive. I've had them when I've had operations, and they barely affect my pain level, make me itchy and uncomfortable, make me stupid. So I could never get addicted to alcohol or opioids; it's just impossible because my biochemistry just does not have that. But I love tasty food. I'm addicted to tasty food. I have a whole bar of alcohol; it's there for decoration. I've never done shrooms basically. I feel the same way about alcohol; I could easily quit. I'll go weeks without having a drink and it doesn't bother me at all. But I know some people, they have one drink and they're...
turns.zh: 是的，因为这真的取决于个人生化特性。对我来说，阿片类药物不会上瘾。我手术时用过，它们几乎不影响我的疼痛程度，让我发痒、不舒服，让我变笨。所以我永远不可能对酒精或阿片类药物上瘾；这根本不可能，因为我的生化特性没有那个倾向。但我喜欢美味的食物。我对美味的食物上瘾。我有一整柜的酒；它们只是装饰。我基本上没试过迷幻蘑菇。我对酒精的感觉也一样；我可以轻松戒掉。我可以几周不喝酒，完全没问题。但我知道有些人，他们喝一杯就……

turns.en: Off to the Races and that's the difference in the biochemical differences that we all have.
turns.zh: 说到赛马，这就是我们所有人之间的生化差异。

turns.en: Yeah, I mean, I think that's the case with a lot of addictions. I'm not addicted to gambling, but I get it. I see it, I've seen it in people. But I have this aversion to things that I know are going to ruin my life. Like, I see it, that's why I've never tried cocaine. I just saw too many people, it looks too fun. I don't want to get involved.
turns.zh: 是的，我觉得很多成瘾都是这样。我不赌，但我能理解。我见过别人那样。但我会本能地回避那些我知道会毁掉我生活的东西。比如，我见过太多人碰可卡因，它看起来太诱人了，我不想沾上。

turns.en: Yeah, I mean, I think generally for any given drug, legal or illegal, the question is: can you complete the following sentence? "Blank made me a better person." Like, I've never heard anyone say meth made them a better person, or cocaine made them a better person. No.
turns.zh: 是的，我觉得对于任何药物，无论合法还是非法，问题在于：你能完成下面这个句子吗？“______ 让我成为了一个更好的人。”我从没听人说过冰毒让他们成为更好的人，或者可卡因让他们成为更好的人。没有。

turns.en: Ever. Um, meth made a lot of soldiers better, I think. That's yeah. I mean, if you're doing, if your soldiers need to stay awake for three days in a row, yeah, it really is effective at that.
turns.zh: 从来没有。嗯，冰毒让很多士兵变得更好了，我觉得。是的，我是说，如果你需要，如果你的士兵需要连续三天不睡觉，那确实很有效。

turns.en: Yeah. People give France a hard time about capitulating in World War II, but you know what's worse than the Nazis? Nazis on meth. They're not stopping, man. Six bullets, they're still coming. That book over there, Blitzed, is all about the use of methamphetamines and the different drugs that they gave their soldiers. The guys at the front of the line, they gave the most meth. Yes, they had different dosages.
turns.zh: 是的。人们总嘲笑法国在二战中投降，但你知道比纳粹更可怕的是什么吗？磕了冰毒的纳粹。他们根本停不下来，老兄。中了六枪还在往前冲。那边那本书《闪电战》讲的就是他们给士兵用甲基苯丙胺和各种药物的情况。排在最前面的士兵拿到的冰毒最多。是的，剂量不同。

turns.en: Yeah, I mean, you just basically think you're invulnerable on meth. And so, it's one thing, like I said, it's one thing to have the Nazis coming after you, but Nazis on meth, you're like, "Holy shit, these guys are not stopping for three days." They're not stopping. It's so crazy.
turns.zh: 是的，我的意思是，磕了冰毒你基本上会觉得自己刀枪不入。所以，就像我说的，纳粹追你是一回事，但磕了冰毒的纳粹，你会想：“天哪，这帮家伙三天都不带停的。”他们根本停不下来。太疯狂了。

turns.en: Yeah, that's not a statement, "Meth made me a better person," that you hear very often. I've never heard that before. No. You hear a lot of like psilocybin advocates, you hear a lot of people that talk about psychedelics. I've actually heard many people say that LSD or mushrooms or MDMA made them a better person. Many people, yeah.
turns.zh: 是的，“冰毒让我成为更好的人”这种话可不常听到。我从没听过。没有。但你听到很多裸盖菇素的倡导者，很多人谈论迷幻药。我确实听过很多人说 LSD 或蘑菇或 MDMA 让他们成为更好的人。很多人，是的。

turns.en: So that's why I'm like, I think a rule for the FDA should be: "Hey look, if you can complete the sentence, legal or illegal, that 'blank made you a better person,' then you got a good drug. And if you can't, you got a bad drug." Also, if there are drugs that are available right now that can absolutely ruin people's lives, the rationalization for stopping other drugs that might ruin people's lives but also can help a lot of people's lives doesn't make any sense, right? You're basically doing the same thing as censorship. You're taking away people's ability to discern what's true and not true, and you're taking away people's ability to discern what's good for you and not good for you. And the way to find that out is to have as much information as possible, so to do research and actually to have unbiased, actual objective observers who are looking at all this stuff that give you real data.
turns.zh: 所以我觉得，FDA 应该有一条规则：“听着，如果你能完成这个句子，无论合法还是非法，‘______ 让我成为了一个更好的人’，那这就是好药。如果不能，那就是坏药。”另外，如果现在有些药物能彻底毁掉人们的生活，那么用这个理由去阻止其他可能毁掉生活但也能帮助很多人的药物，这根本说不通，对吧？这基本上和审查制度一样。你在剥夺人们辨别真假的能力，也在剥夺人们判断什么对自己好、什么不好的能力。而找到答案的方法就是获取尽可能多的信息，去做研究，并且要有不带偏见的、客观的观察者来看待这一切，给你真实的数据。

turns.en: Yes. And the opposite of that, or the consequence of that, is if you don't do that, you're empowering cartels. Yes, that's the whole reason why they have all that money. It's because it's illegal to sell these drugs in America. The demand is never going away. So instead of limiting the amount of drugs, now you've got toxic drugs because fentanyl and all this other stuff has been cut in because they're not pure. So you're just killing people. You're not saving anybody by protecting them from themselves.
turns.zh: 是的。而相反的情况，或者说后果，就是如果你不这么做，你就是在壮大贩毒集团。是的，这就是他们有钱的全部原因。因为在美国卖这些药是违法的。需求永远不会消失。所以现在你不仅没有限制毒品数量，反而因为掺了芬太尼之类的杂质，毒品变得有毒。你只是在杀人。通过保护人们免受自身伤害，你并没有拯救任何人。

turns.en: True. But it's a tricky situation because what do you do? Like if you just say, "Okay, now everyone can sell," all these people that have been selling boner pills, now you can sell meth. Like, holy shit, you get the double combo: the Viagra and meth. Right? Jesus Christ.
turns.zh: 没错。但这是个棘手的情况，因为你能怎么办？如果你直接说：“好了，现在谁都可以卖”，那些一直卖壮阳药的人现在也能卖冰毒了。天哪，你还能来个双组合：伟哥加冰毒。对吧？老天爷。

turns.en: Oh my god. Well, I mean, how many people are already doing that right now? There's a lot of people out there that are essentially on meth, especially people that abuse Adderall. They're basically amphetamines up all day long. Yeah, Adderall is low-grade methamphetamine.
turns.zh: 我的天。嗯，我是说，现在有多少人已经在这么干了？外面很多人本质上就是在用冰毒，尤其是那些滥用阿德拉的人。他们基本上整天都在用安非他命。是的，阿德拉就是低配版的甲基苯丙胺。

turns.en: Yeah. And I've actually seen people become much worse people if they take too much Adderall. Much worse, you know? It's like an anger amplifier. So, now I'm not saying Adderall is something where there are pluses and minuses. It's not a clear-cut issue. It does help some people a great deal. But in higher doses, man, that stuff, I've seen people turn into just raging monsters on high doses of Adderall. They're just angry, extremely angry all the time. Yeah, they're messed up.
turns.zh: 是的。我确实见过有人吃太多阿德拉后变得更糟。糟得多，你知道吗？就像愤怒放大器。所以，我不是说阿德拉没有利弊。这不是一个非黑即白的问题。它对有些人确实帮助很大。但高剂量下，老兄，那东西，我见过有人在高剂量阿德拉下变成了狂暴的怪物。他们就是愤怒，一直极度愤怒。是的，他们完蛋了。

turns.en: Yeah, that's what happens if you take meth. It's crazy. Meth turns you into a freaking rage demon. And so many prescriptions, I'm like, Jesus. We googled it, one year there was like 39 million prescriptions for Adderall in this country. Oh yeah. Like once in a while there's an Adderall shortage and there's like widespread panic, you know? And then what do people do? And then it's the same thing as when they tried to limit the amount of OxyContin, people go to street heroin. And if you're addicted to Adderall and your dealer, the guy who sells you weed, is like, "Hey man, I can get you low-grade meth, like the stuff the Nazis took." Well, they had high-grade meth actually. They had pharmaceutical grade. It was epic meth. It was like made by the... Pharmaceutical grade meth is going to be... If you look at the freaking Wikipedia page, there are many different versions of meth, not all the same, and they have different effects. But pharmaceutical grade pure meth, you are going to be, oh my God, super productive for a certain period of time, and you're not going to sleep for a while, and then you will have some anger management issues.
turns.zh: 是的，这就是磕冰毒的下场。太疯狂了。冰毒把你变成一个该死的愤怒恶魔。而且处方量这么大，我真是服了。我们谷歌过，有一年这个国家开出了 3900 万张阿德拉处方。哦，是的。偶尔阿德拉短缺，就会引发大范围恐慌，你知道吗？然后人们怎么办？这和当年试图限制奥施康定用量时一样，人们转向街头海洛因。如果你对阿德拉上瘾，而你的毒贩，那个卖你大麻的家伙，说：“嘿，老兄，我能搞到低配冰毒，就是纳粹用的那种。”其实他们用的是高纯度冰毒。是药用级别的。那是史诗级的冰毒。就像……药用级纯冰毒会让你，哦我的天，在一段时间内超级高效，你会好一阵子不睡觉，然后就会出现一些愤怒管理问题。

turns.en: So, they actually, the Nazis did actually roll back how much meth they were using because they had quite a few incidents of the soldiers killing their officers because they were on too much meth. Jesus Christ. They would shoot them. Many officers got fragged by their platoon that was on too much meth. That happened quite a few times. You just, when someone's on a lot of meth, they can get very angry.
turns.zh: 所以，实际上，纳粹确实减少了冰毒的使用量，因为他们发生了好几起士兵因服用过量冰毒而杀死军官的事件。天哪。他们会开枪。很多军官被自己磕了太多冰毒的排给干掉了。这种事发生了好几次。一个人磕了大量冰毒后，会变得非常愤怒。

turns.en: Did you ever pay attention to when John McAfee was cooking meth in a lab in his backyard? I mean, McAfee is quite a character. He was a character. We had him on the podcast when he was on the run. So he called in from an undisclosed location when he was running from... Where was he? Costa Rica? Is that where he was? Belize, right? So when he was running from the authorities, he called in. We had him on the podcast on the run. And I was asking him about these posts because there was an online account that was linked to him where he had this very detailed laboratory...
turns.zh: 你有没有注意过约翰·麦卡菲在他后院实验室里制冰毒的事？我是说，麦卡菲真是个奇人。他是个奇人。他逃亡的时候我们请他上过播客。他从一个秘密地点打进来，当时他在躲避……他在哪儿？哥斯达黎加？是那儿吗？伯利兹，对吧？所以他在躲避当局的时候打进来，我们在他逃亡期间请他上了播客。我问他关于那些帖子的事，因为有一个和他关联的在线账号，上面有他非常详细的实验室……

turns.en: Sophisticated making the best math like a super genius cooking math. I mean, I think he had his lab, making a wide range of drugs. And I talked to a reporter who went down and interviewed him in Bise, and the reporter said that's one of the scariest things. He was quite terrified. So one of the things Mafi had, I guess this trick where he would play Russian roulette with himself. He'd put a bullet in the revolver, spin the chamber, and clearly he had some trick to know it's not the right bullet. But I do wonder if Mafi is high and he does that, he's not always going to get the trick right.
turns.zh: 他做数学就像超级天才一样。我是说，我觉得他有自己的实验室，制造各种毒品。我跟一个记者聊过，他去比斯采访了马菲，记者说那是他见过最吓人的事之一。他吓坏了。马菲有个把戏，就是跟自己玩俄罗斯轮盘赌。他往左轮手枪里放一颗子弹，转动弹膛，显然他有某种技巧知道那不是要命的子弹。但我在想，如果马菲嗨了还这么干，他不可能每次都把技巧用对吧。

turns.en: Do you sure he had a trick?
turns.zh: 你确定他有技巧？

turns.en: So according to this reporter, when he went to visit Mafi in Bise, Mafi took out the revolver, put a bullet in, spun the chamber, and then pointed at his head and went click. The reporter is like, "Please don't do this, this is insane." Click, click, click. And then pointed the gun at the ground and went click, bang, and shot a bullet in the ground.
turns.zh: 据这个记者说，他去比斯拜访马菲时，马菲拿出左轮手枪，装了一颗子弹，转动弹膛，然后对准自己的头扣了扳机，咔哒一声。记者说：“求你别这样，这太疯狂了。”咔哒、咔哒、咔哒。然后他把枪对准地面，咔哒一声，砰，朝地上开了一枪。

turns.en: Jesus, that's a hell of a party trick. Jeez, it's the next level party trick.
turns.zh: 天哪，这派对把戏也太绝了。真是顶级派对把戏。

turns.en: That's the guy who seen The Deer Hunter too many times. Remember that scene when they were forcing... yes, that was a heavy scene. De Niro and Christopher Walken, that's one of the greatest scenes in any movie ever. I remember watching that scene just clawing at my pants.
turns.zh: 这家伙《猎鹿人》看太多了。记得那场戏吗？他们强迫……是的，那场戏很沉重。德尼罗和克里斯托弗·沃肯，那是电影史上最伟大的场景之一。我记得看那场戏时紧张得抓裤子。

turns.en: Mafi was a wild boy. Wild. And you created brilliant antivirus software.
turns.zh: 马菲是个野小子。太野了。而且他还创造了出色的杀毒软件。

turns.en: He may have made some of the viruses too.
turns.zh: 他可能也制造了一些病毒。

turns.en: You think so? Well, didn't he give laptops to a bunch of government organizations with viruses on them, so that he could pay attention to what they were doing?
turns.zh: 你这么想？他不是给一堆政府机构送了带病毒的笔记本电脑，好让他能监视他们在干什么吗？

turns.en: I wouldn't be surprised if somebody whacked that guy. I don't know what happened to him, but he would be a guy that would be like, "This guy is a little bit too loose." He probably had sensitive information. I don't know for sure he did. I found him to be an interesting guy. I generally feel like if somebody's not harming someone else, they should be okay. Now there is some suggestion that Mafi killed his neighbor in Bise.
turns.zh: 如果有人干掉那家伙，我不会惊讶。我不知道他后来怎么样了，但他就是那种会让人说“这家伙太不靠谱了”的人。他很可能掌握敏感信息。我不确定他有没有。我觉得他是个有趣的人。我一般觉得，只要不伤害别人，就没什么问题。不过有说法称马菲在比斯杀了他的邻居。

turns.en: Probably did. Maybe the neighbor was a douchebag. I think he probably did. Seems like he probably did. The neighbor killed his dog, and then it seems like he killed the neighbor. Allegedly. I mean, it seems likely. It's not a zero possibility. It's definitely not zero. It seems more than not. He's a methed-up wild man playing Russian roulette. Hey, maybe you kill your neighbor. I mean, if somebody killed your dog, you'd be really inclined to kill them too.
turns.zh: 很可能干了。也许邻居是个混蛋。我觉得他很可能干了。看起来他很可能干了。邻居杀了他的狗，然后他好像杀了邻居。据说是这样。我是说，看起来很有可能。可能性不是零。绝对不是零。可能性大于一半。他是个嗑药的野人，玩俄罗斯轮盘。嘿，也许你会杀了邻居。我是说，如果有人杀了你的狗，你也很可能想杀了他。

turns.en: Somebody killed your squirrel, John Wick. Yeah, the squirrel thing is bananas.
turns.zh: 有人杀了你的松鼠，约翰·威克。是啊，松鼠那事太离谱了。

turns.en: So here's the thing about the whole squirrel thing. How can it be that we live in America, supposedly land of the free, and the government can barge into your home with guns, so if you resist you're going to get shot, and then take your pets and execute them? If they can do that to your pets, what do you think they can do to you? I know that's not an exaggeration. It sounds like you're... oh, that's so crazy, how can you make that connection? But it's... why would you kill that cute little squirrel that was obviously a pet and trained from the time it was a baby? If you see the interaction that guy has with that squirrel, it was wonderful. It was really cute. Yes, absolutely. It was just obviously a pet squirrel, and raccoon too, and doing no harm. And the government comes in, barges into the guy's house, takes his pets, and kills them. I think this should really get people mobilized frankly, because you think you see like the John Wick movie where John Wick just wants peace. In the movie, he just wants to retire, and they offer him tons of money because they want him to keep being an assassin. They threaten him. He's like, "Listen, I'm out." And they kill his dog. That was a bad idea. They killed a cute little puppy, and the puppy was his ex-wife's gift to him when she died of cancer. Great movie. The best revenge movie of all time, because it's so ridiculous. He kills everybody. And you're rooting for him. They shouldn't have killed his dog. They shouldn't have killed that squirrel. How many cases have we not heard about? Look at that little guy. That squirrel clearly had a love relationship with that guy. He would hop all over him and climb on him. That was his pet. That squirrel thought of that man as his protector, his companion. There was nothing wrong with that. And in Texas, it's totally legal. You can have a zebra out here. You can have whatever you want. And that's the argument for freedom. The flip side is you get a bunch of people with tigers in their backyard, which is not great. But this was a squirrel. It's not an anaconda or a crocodile or a chimpanzee.
turns.zh: 所以关于整个松鼠事件，事情是这样的：我们生活在美国，号称自由之地，政府怎么能持枪闯入你家，如果你反抗就会被枪杀，然后带走你的宠物并处决？如果他们能这样对待你的宠物，你觉得他们能对你做什么？我知道这不是夸张。听起来好像……哦，这太疯狂了，你怎么能这么联系？但问题是……为什么要杀死那只可爱的小松鼠？它明显是宠物，从小养大的。如果你看到那家伙和松鼠的互动，那太棒了。真的很可爱。是的，绝对。那明显是一只宠物松鼠，还有浣熊，没有造成任何伤害。政府闯进他家，带走他的宠物并杀死它们。我认为这真的应该让人们行动起来，因为你想，就像《疾速追杀》里，约翰·威克只想要平静。电影里，他只想退休，他们给他很多钱，因为他们想让他继续当杀手。他们威胁他。他说：“听着，我不干了。”然后他们杀了他的狗。那是个坏主意。他们杀了一只可爱的小狗，而那只狗是他前妻死于癌症时留给他的礼物。好电影。有史以来最好的复仇电影，因为它太荒谬了。他杀了所有人。你还支持他。他们不该杀他的狗。他们不该杀那只松鼠。我们没听说过的案子还有多少？看看那个小家伙。那只松鼠显然和那家伙有感情。它会跳到他身上，爬到他身上。那是他的宠物。那只松鼠把那个人当作保护者、伙伴。这没什么不对。在得克萨斯州，这完全合法。你可以养斑马。你想要什么都可以。这就是自由的论点。反面是很多人后院养老虎，这不好。但这只是松鼠，不是蟒蛇、鳄鱼或黑猩猩。

turns.en: Did you see Chimp Crazy? Oh man, chimps will eat your face. They will mess you up. They don't even kill you, they just... chimps don't even kill people, which is really weird. They just bite your hands off and rip your dick off and tear your face apart. They want to leave you alive. They could kill you easily if a chimp wanted to just punch you in the head until you're dead, it wouldn't take long. But they don't kill you, they just rip you apart. And you can have a chimp. Well, you used to be able to have a chimp in a lot of states, and then Chimp Crazy kind of exposed a lot of that, and PETA did a great job of stopping people from keeping chimps as pets, because once they hit like five, you can't control them anymore. That's totally understandable. If somebody has a creature that is dangerous to others, but obviously a squirrel and a raccoon are not. Squirrels are everywhere. That's what's so crazy. Why can't you have it in the house? What kind of rules are we dealing with? You have rats everywhere. I mean, they're allowing criminals to go free, violent criminals to go free, but they're spending your tax dollars to come in and execute your pets. What the hell?
turns.zh: 你看过《疯狂黑猩猩》吗？哦天哪，黑猩猩会吃掉你的脸。它们会把你搞残。它们甚至不杀你，它们只是……黑猩猩甚至不杀人，这真的很奇怪。它们只是咬掉你的手，扯掉你的命根子，撕烂你的脸。它们想让你活着。如果黑猩猩想一拳把你打死，很容易，用不了多久。但它们不杀你，它们只是把你撕碎。而且你可以养黑猩猩。嗯，以前很多州都可以养，后来《疯狂黑猩猩》揭露了很多问题，PETA 在阻止人们养黑猩猩方面做得很好，因为一旦它们长到五岁左右，你就控制不住了。这完全可以理解。如果有人养了危险的动物，但松鼠和浣熊显然不是。松鼠到处都是。这就是最疯狂的地方。为什么不能在家里养？我们面对的是什么规则？到处都有老鼠。我是说，他们让罪犯逍遥法外，暴力罪犯逍遥法外，却花你的税金闯进来处决你的宠物。搞什么？

turns.en: What is going on exactly? And it's like, but it's overreach, it's government overreach, and this just keeps getting worse every year. And that's why we've got to fight back against this. And people say like, well, it's just a squirrel. Well, was it, you know, in John Wick's case, it was just a dog, right? Yeah, you know, but remember the Russian guy said it's a dog, it's just a dog. Just a squirrel. Yeah, well, the funniest thing is, I just don't understand how anybody could justify it. It seems to me that in a logical world, all that guy would have to do is say, you see me with this squirrel? This squirrel's a pet. Yeah, like look, he hops on me, he eats, he sleeps. I can keep a gerbil, but I can't keep a squirrel. I can have a guinea pig, I can't have a squirrel. I can have a chinchilla. My daughter has a chinchilla, it's adorable, adorable little thing, climbs all over. Can't have a squirrel. Even if they did take a squirrel away, couldn't they have released it into the woods or something? Well, the idea is you have to euthanize it because it's used to being fed, it doesn't know how to forage, it won't be able to find a home. Squirrels are brutal, squirrels are absolutely brutal to each other. They throw each other out of trees, which is one of the reasons why squirrels can fall from like 30 feet and just bounce off the ground and live. It's a natural adaptation because squirrels during mating bite each other. There used to be a rumor, a myth, that squirrels bite each other's nuts off. Okay, that seems to be a myth, but it came from the fact that squirrels are so ruthless during mating. So one female is just running away. I have squirrels in my backyard, I watch it all the time. One female apparently goes into estrus, and all the male squirrels fight to get to her. So they're running up trees and chasing each other around trees, literally throwing each other off trees to try to have... So if this poor little Peanut the squirrel, who's used to living with a guy in an apartment, gets out there in the wild world, well, fair enough, at least they have a chance. Yeah, at least he has a chance. But chance? How about just leave him with the guy? Yeah, leave him with the guy for sure. What the hell is wrong with you? Why are you killing that squirrel? It doesn't make any sense. Yeah, and then to add insult to injury, there were a bunch of people on the left who were actually posting that they're glad that MAGA squirrel got killed. Which is MAGA squirrel? Like the squirrel has an ideology. It's a cute little fluffy squirrel. Exactly. Well, it's a nice symbol because most reasonable, compassionate people think that's terrible, and most people who have pets think it's terrible. So I don't know. I hope people just go out there and vote for Peanut man. If nothing else, just vote for Peanut.
turns.zh: 到底发生了什么？这简直是越权，是政府越权，而且每年都在恶化。这就是为什么我们必须反击。有人说，嗯，那只是一只松鼠。但你看，在《疾速追杀》里，那只是一条狗，对吧？是啊，但记得那个俄罗斯人说，那是条狗，只是条狗。只是只松鼠。是啊，最搞笑的是，我完全无法理解怎么会有人能为此辩护。在我看来，在一个理性的世界里，那个人只需要说：你看到我和这只松鼠了吗？它是宠物。你看，它跳到我身上，它吃、它睡。我能养沙鼠，但不能养松鼠。我能养豚鼠，但不能养松鼠。我能养龙猫。我女儿有只龙猫，很可爱，到处爬。但不能养松鼠。就算他们把松鼠带走，难道不能把它放回树林里吗？但问题是，你必须对它实施安乐死，因为它习惯了被喂食，不知道如何觅食，也找不到家。松鼠很残忍，它们对彼此极其残忍。它们把对方扔下树，这也是为什么松鼠能从 30 英尺高处摔下来，弹一下地面就能活下来。这是一种自然适应，因为松鼠在交配时会互相撕咬。以前有个谣言，说松鼠会咬掉对方的蛋蛋。好吧，那似乎是个谣言，但源于松鼠在交配时极其无情。所以一只雌松鼠在逃跑。我家后院有松鼠，我经常看到。一只雌松鼠发情了，所有雄松鼠都争着去找她。它们爬上树，绕着树追逐，真的把对方扔下树，就为了……所以如果这只可怜的小花生，习惯了和一个人住在公寓里，到了野外，好吧，至少它们有机会。是啊，至少有机会。但机会？为什么不直接把它留给那个人？是啊，肯定该留给那个人。你到底有什么毛病？为什么要杀那只松鼠？完全没道理。是啊，更糟的是，一些左派人士竟然发帖说他们很高兴 MAGA 松鼠被杀。MAGA 松鼠？好像松鼠有意识形态似的。它只是只可爱的小毛球。没错。嗯，这是个很好的象征，因为大多数理性、有同情心的人都觉得这很糟糕，大多数养宠物的人也觉得很糟糕。所以我不知道。我希望人们出去投票给花生男。就算别的都不管，就投票给花生。

turns.en: They've done such a job of painting Trump as a monster. They've taken the worst things that he's ever said and amplified them. He's not a perfect person, but guess what, no one's a perfect person. They don't exist. This purity test. Like if Obama was a perfect person, he wouldn't be lying on stage about that very fine people hoax. There's exactly no one who's going to be a perfect person. But the thing that they didn't understand about Trump is he's so crazy that if you tell him he can't be president, like remember Obama did that during the White House Correspondents' Dinner? There's one thing that I am that you'll never be: president of the United States. You see Trump in the audience going, okay. Funny thing is, I was actually at that White House Correspondents' Dinner. It's supposed to be a roast of the president, right? Trump's there, he's actually supporting, you know, basically if you go to the White House Correspondents' Dinner, you're there in support of the president and support of the press. And it's meant to be roasting the president. Trump's just there, he's like, you know, just he's there as part of the support. And then they turned it around and just started roasting Trump. And he's just sitting there. I'm like, he's like, yo, I just came to the dinner, I wasn't, I'm just here to support. You know what it was because of, right? The birther stuff. Oh, okay, yeah, that's what it all was. Trump was at the head of a lot of these people spreading this rumor online that Obama's birth certificate was forged and he's actually from Kenya. What's weird is if you go back to Obama's early days, there are some things that say he's from Kenya. Like I think in his college something said he was from Kenya. But you know, that could just be people print things wrong all the time. It doesn't mean he's actually from Kenya. But Trump was one of those guys that was like spreading that supposedly false rumor. Why is he pushing it hard? I'm not, this is the kind of thing where I want to just go and look at saying, what did he actually say? No, he definitely was. He was definitely saying, you know, look, he I don't think he has the time to go into things very deeply. Yeah, and so I think he could probably be influenced by a bunch of people like these Marjorie Taylor Greene type people who come to him with some wild-ass theory. True, he might be. And I think there's a lot of that stuff that gets fed to people on purpose so that they'll say incorrect things so that they're easy to dismiss. And I think there's also a lot of people that just make up, and you know, they tell you the Earth is flat, and then a bunch of people watch a YouTube video and they believe it. Yeah, well, but on that White House Correspondents' Dinner, I was there, and the degree to which they attacked Trump in that dinner was really over the top. It was making everyone uncomfortable. Really, it was really over the top. You know, I mean, I think like a passing joke or a few passing jokes are fine, but they twisted the knife big on Trump in that, and you could see Trump just getting angrier and angrier and more and more upset. I wonder if, and it's like, man, this is not good karma. That's what I was thinking at the time. I was two tables away from Trump, and I'm looking, I'm like, man, this is too much. Well, it's kind of crazy what they made out of that, because that's the kind of guy that if you tell him he can't do something, he's going to just keep trying. What a big mistake to rag on him so much with that White House Correspondents' Dinner. Well, just look at the way they've attacked him in terms of using the legal system, like this thing in New York where the 34 different felony counts.
turns.zh: 他们把特朗普描绘成一个怪物。他们把他说过的最糟糕的话放大。他不是个完美的人，但猜怎么着，没有人是完美的。不存在完美的人。这种纯洁性测试。如果奥巴马是完美的人，他就不会在台上撒谎，说什么“非常优秀的人”骗局。没有人会是完美的人。但他们不明白特朗普的一点是，他太疯狂了，如果你告诉他他不能当总统，就像奥巴马在白宫记者协会晚宴上做的那样？有一件事我是而你永远成不了：美国总统。你看到特朗普在观众席上，好吧。有趣的是，我确实参加了那次白宫记者协会晚宴。那本来应该是吐槽总统的，对吧？特朗普在那里，他实际上是来支持的，基本上如果你去白宫记者协会晚宴，你是来支持总统和媒体的。而且应该是吐槽总统。特朗普就在那里，他是来支持的。然后他们反过来开始吐槽特朗普。他就坐在那里。我想，喂，我只是来参加晚宴，我不是……我只是来支持的。你知道那是为什么吗？因为出生地问题。哦，好吧，是的，就是那个。特朗普是很多在网上散布谣言的人的头儿，说奥巴马的出生证明是伪造的，他实际上来自肯尼亚。奇怪的是，如果你回顾奥巴马的早期，有些资料说他来自肯尼亚。比如他大学里的什么东西说他来自肯尼亚。但你知道，那可能只是人们经常印错东西。并不意味着他真的来自肯尼亚。但特朗普是散布那个所谓假谣言的人之一。他为什么那么卖力地推动？我不是……这种事我想去查一下，他到底说了什么？不，他确实说了。他确实在说，你看，我觉得他没有时间深入了解事情。是啊，所以我认为他可能被一群像玛乔丽·泰勒·格林那样的人影响，他们带着一些疯狂的理论来找他。没错，他可能确实如此。而且我认为很多那样的东西是故意喂给人们的，这样他们就会说错话，从而容易被驳斥。而且我认为也有很多人就是编造，他们告诉你地球是平的，然后一群人看了 YouTube 视频就信了。是啊，但在那次白宫记者协会晚宴上，我在场，他们攻击特朗普的程度真的太过分了。让所有人都感到不舒服。真的，太过分了。我的意思是，我觉得开一两个玩笑没问题，但他们在那次晚宴上对特朗普下了狠手，你可以看到特朗普越来越生气，越来越沮丧。我想，伙计，这不是好兆头。我当时就是这么想的。我离特朗普两桌远，我看着，心想，这太过分了。他们从那次晚宴中搞出来的东西真是疯狂，因为他是那种如果你告诉他他不能做某事，他就会一直尝试的人。在那次白宫记者协会晚宴上那么挖苦他真是个巨大的错误。看看他们利用法律体系攻击他的方式，比如纽约那件事，34 项重罪指控。

turns.en: They were essentially misdemeanors, bookkeeping errors. They decided to try him for these even though the statute of limitations had passed. They didn't identify a felony. Abuse of the law is what's going on. But most people would have quit. After the E. Jean Carroll lawsuit, this lawsuit, the insurrection thing, the Georgia thing, getting kicked off Twitter—most people would say, 'This is too much, I can't take this.' But he's so crazy, he's like, 'All right, come on, we're going to war.' He just digs his heels in and keeps going.
turns.zh: 这些本质上都是轻罪，是记账错误。他们决定起诉他，即使诉讼时效已过。他们没有认定重罪。这是滥用法律。但大多数人早就放弃了。在 E. Jean Carroll 诉讼、这个诉讼、叛乱事件、佐治亚事件、被推特封号之后——大多数人会说，‘这太过分了，我受不了了。’但他太疯狂了，他说，‘好吧，来吧，我们要开战。’他只是坚持己见，继续前进。

turns.en: Yeah, it's the wrong guy to do that to. Just attacking him at the White House Correspondents' Dinner—most people would have been humiliated. He got angry, he's like, 'Yeah, all right, say I'll be president. I've been thinking about running for about 15 years. Finally, I'm going to run.'
turns.zh: 是的，他们找错了对象。在白宫记者晚宴上攻击他——大多数人会感到羞辱。他生气了，他说，‘好吧，我会当总统。我已经考虑竞选大约 15 年了。终于，我要参选了。’

turns.en: Yeah, that was a real bad move. But I can certainly understand making some jokes, a few passing jokes on Trump. But man, I was there at that dinner, and they ragged on Trump so much, it was insane.
turns.zh: 是的，那真是糟糕的一步。但我当然能理解开些玩笑，对特朗普开几个随口的玩笑。但天哪，我当时在那场晚宴上，他们拿特朗普开涮得太厉害了，简直疯了。

turns.en: The reason I would push back on that is there were a bunch of different speakers, right? Trump would obviously be a target. If they all attacked him, it's because he's... if you're going to make fun of people in the audience, especially the zeitgeist, that whole birther thing was big. Most people dismissed it as a ridiculous conspiracy theory. So who is this guy saying this? So you have eight to ten individual speakers writing monologues. Of course they're all going to hit Trump.
turns.zh: 我之所以反驳，是因为有很多不同的演讲者，对吧？特朗普显然会成为目标。如果他们都攻击他，那是因为他……如果你要取笑观众，尤其是时代精神，整个出生地阴谋论当时很热门。大多数人认为那是个荒谬的阴谋论。所以这家伙是谁，说这种话？所以有八到十个演讲者在写独白。他们当然都会攻击特朗普。

turns.en: Yeah, well, obviously it was a mistake. They shouldn't have done that. But if you buy people to watch that original source material, I think a few jokes are fine. But it felt like he was the primary object of the roast, which is not the whole point. It's a roast of the president, not a roast of the audience.
turns.zh: 是的，显然是个错误。他们不应该那样做。但如果你让人们看原始素材，我觉得开几个玩笑没问题。但感觉他是被调侃的主要对象，这不是重点。这是对总统的调侃，不是对观众的调侃。

turns.en: The thing about it is, he's easy to roast. And on top of that, Obama was loved and cherished by the left. Most of those people are on the left. There's only so far you can push. You can't ask him about a chef. You know, like, 'What happened with the chef, bro?' You can't bring up certain things. 'What's your favorite sport? Paddle boarding?' Wasn't that guy a really good swimmer? Tell me what happened. You can't bring that up. If you're going to roast Hillary, you can't bring up the death count. Like, 'Hillary, what's the best way to stay in touch? Email?' If you're doing one of those, you know, she destroyed the servers and poured bleach on them. Like computers. She poured bleach on them. That's what I saw. It wasn't just like they took a hammer to it. They destroyed it so there was no possible way to get forensics on it. What was in there? That's what I mean. What was in there? Why would they care so much? That's so crazy.
turns.zh: 问题是，他很容易被调侃。而且，奥巴马深受左派喜爱。那些人大多是左派。你能调侃的程度有限。你不能问他关于厨师的事。你知道，就像，‘那个厨师怎么了，兄弟？’有些事你不能提。‘你最喜欢的运动是什么？桨板？’那家伙不是游泳很厉害吗？告诉我发生了什么。你不能提那个。如果你要调侃希拉里，你不能提死亡人数。比如，‘希拉里，保持联系的最好方式是什么？电子邮件？’如果你做那种事，你知道，她销毁了服务器，还倒了漂白剂。就像电脑一样。她倒了漂白剂。我亲眼所见。不只是用锤子砸。他们销毁了它，以至于完全无法进行取证。里面有什么？我就是这个意思。里面有什么？他们为什么那么在意？太疯狂了。

turns.en: Yeah, the whole thing is there was no legal action against that, which is clear destruction of evidence.
turns.zh: 是的，整件事就是没有针对那件事采取法律行动，这显然是销毁证据。

turns.en: Well, it's also this other narrative that always drives me crazy: that he's going to destroy democracy. So in order to destroy democracy, we have to install a president without a primary. We have to have a candidate that is the least liked vice president of all time, the least popular vice president of all time, and then use gaslighting and the full force of the media machine to turn her into the future and hope. And then we're going to... this she's going to be change, even though she's a sitting vice president. And on top of that, this idea of change when the Democrats have been in control for what, 12 or 16 years? Right, which is crazy. Like, this is the change?
turns.zh: 嗯，还有另一个说法总是让我抓狂：他要摧毁民主。所以为了摧毁民主，我们必须在没有初选的情况下任命一位总统。我们必须有一个有史以来最不受欢迎的副总统候选人，有史以来最不受欢迎的副总统，然后利用煤气灯效应和媒体机器的全力把她塑造成未来和希望。然后我们要……她将带来改变，尽管她是在任副总统。而且，当民主党已经掌权 12 或 16 年的时候，这种改变的想法？对吧，这太疯狂了。就像，这就是改变？

turns.en: Yeah, I mean, obviously I view this election as a turning point, like a fork in the road of destiny that is incredibly important. I've not been politically active until this election. The reason I've been politically active this election is because I think if we don't elect Trump, we will lose democracy in this country. We will lose the two-party system. Let me explain why. There are only six or seven swing states. The margin of victory in those states is small, often like 10 or 20,000 votes. What the Democrat administration has been doing is importing vast numbers of illegals into swing states. You can look at the numbers on the actual government website. You don't have to take my word for it. Just look at the numbers as reported by the government, which is controlled by the Democrats. What we've seen is triple-digit increases in the number of illegals in every swing state. In some cases, 700% increases. These are gigantic numbers. So if you have a state that had a 10 or 20,000 vote margin, and you put 200,000 illegals into that state, you 10x the margin. You swamp it. It's not a swing state anymore. It's going to vote blue. And once the swing states vote blue, there is no election anymore. There's only a Democrat primary. Which is so crazy, and it's so crazy that people are fine with that.
turns.zh: 是的，我的意思是，显然我把这次选举视为一个转折点，就像命运的分岔路口，极其重要。在这次选举之前，我从未积极参与政治。我这次积极参与政治的原因是我认为如果我们不选特朗普，我们将失去这个国家的民主。我们将失去两党制。让我解释原因。只有六七个摇摆州。这些州的获胜差距很小，通常只有 1 万或 2 万票。民主党政府一直在做的是将大量非法移民引入摇摆州。你可以在政府官方网站上查看数字。你不必相信我的话。只需查看政府报告的数字，而政府是由民主党控制的。我们看到每个摇摆州的非法移民数量都出现了三位数的增长。在某些情况下，增长了 700%。这些数字非常巨大。所以如果你有一个州有 1 万或 2 万票的差距，而你往那个州投入 20 万非法移民，你就把差距扩大了 10 倍。你淹没了它。它不再是摇摆州了。它会投给民主党。一旦摇摆州投给民主党，就不再有什么选举了。只有民主党初选。这太疯狂了，而且人们对此无动于衷，这太疯狂了。

turns.en: Well, I guess people on the left will be fine with that because they think that's a good idea. They just want to win. Correct.
turns.zh: 嗯，我想左派的人会对此无动于衷，因为他们认为这是个好主意。他们只想赢。没错。

turns.en: The thing is, one does not need any grand conspiracy theory for this. You just have to look at the simple matter of incentives. If the Democrat Party wants to win, to achieve permanent victory, all they need to do is turn the swing states blue. They have permanent victory, and then we're a one-party state. Then they will keep doing that, obviously. They will keep stacking the deck by bringing in vast numbers of illegals into the swing states, keep stacking it so that each successive election will be worse than the last. And that's what's happening. If you want to see if this is actually going to happen, look at California. California is super majority Dem, 70% Dem. A month ago, they passed a law making it illegal to show ID in any election in California. So a friend of mine went to vote in Palo Alto because he was like, 'Is this for real?' He tried to show his ID, and they reacted like if you show a cross to a vampire. They're like, 'No, we can't even look at that ID.' It is illegal for them to even look at your ID if you want to present it. In California, for any election at all, even city council. What logical reason, other than to cheat?
turns.zh: 问题是，这不需要任何宏大阴谋论。你只需看看简单的激励因素。如果民主党想要获胜，实现永久胜利，他们所要做的就是让摇摆州变蓝。他们就能永久胜利，然后我们就成了一党制国家。然后他们显然会继续这样做。他们会继续通过将大量非法移民引入摇摆州来操纵局势，不断加码，使每次选举都比上一次更糟。这就是正在发生的事情。如果你想看看这是否真的会发生，看看加州。加州是绝对多数民主党，70% 是民主党。一个月前，他们通过了一项法律，规定在加州任何选举中出示身份证都是非法的。所以我一个朋友去帕洛阿尔托投票，他心想，‘这是真的吗？’他试图出示身份证，他们的反应就像你向吸血鬼展示十字架一样。他们说，‘不，我们甚至不能看那个身份证。’即使你想出示，他们看你的身份证也是违法的。在加州，任何选举，甚至市议会选举，都是如此。除了作弊，还有什么合乎逻辑的理由？

turns.en: Would you ever have that law? The reason is to cheat. That's the only argument you can make any other way. And I think 84% of people polled believe that you should show ID to vote, so it's against the will of the people. Yes, and we are extremely aware we're an outlier in not requiring ID. Basically, almost every country on Earth requires ID to vote. So as soon as you ban ID for voting, it makes fraud impossible to prove because how do you trace the fraud? Right. It's insane. It's insane. It's insane. And what I'm saying is, how is it legal? This election is the last chance to preserve democracy in America. Mark my words. Everything they accuse Trump of, they are guilty of. And if Trump doesn't win, this will be the last real election in America. And if the Kamala puppet machine wins, they will legalize the illegals in the swing states. There will be no swing states. Every election going forward will be a guaranteed Democrat win, and it'll actually be worse than California. The reason it'll be worse than California is that the one thing that keeps California from being super crazy is that you can move out of California, like you and I did. We used to be in California, we moved to Texas. We're still in America. But if the Dems win this election, they will legalize enough illegals to turn the swing states, and everywhere will be like California. There will be no escape. That is so insane. This is the final chance. This is it. This is the last chance. Has anybody tried to push? Go out and vote. Vote like your life depends on it. Vote like your future depends on it, because it does. This is the last chance, man.
turns.zh: 你见过那种法律吗？它的目的就是为了作弊。这是你唯一能得出的结论。我认为 84% 的受访者认为投票应该出示身份证，所以这违背了民意。是的，我们非常清楚，不要求身份证是个异类。基本上，地球上每个国家都要求投票时出示身份证。所以一旦你禁止投票时查验身份证，欺诈就无法被证明，因为你如何追踪欺诈？对吧。这太疯狂了。太疯狂了。太疯狂了。我的意思是，这怎么可能是合法的？这次选举是美国民主的最后机会。记住我的话。他们指控特朗普的一切，他们自己都犯了。如果特朗普不赢，这将是美国最后一次真正的选举。如果卡马拉傀儡机器赢了，他们会让摇摆州的非法移民合法化。将不再有摇摆州。以后的每一次选举都将是民主党稳赢，而且会比加州更糟。比加州更糟的原因是，加州之所以还没彻底疯狂，是因为你可以搬出加州，就像你和我做的那样。我们以前在加州，搬到了得克萨斯。我们还在美国。但如果民主党赢了这次选举，他们会把足够多的非法移民合法化，从而改变摇摆州，每个地方都会变得像加州一样。无处可逃。这太疯狂了。这是最后的机会。就是现在。这是最后的机会。有人尝试过推动吗？出去投票。投票，就像你的生命依赖于它。投票，就像你的未来依赖于它，因为它确实如此。这是最后的机会，老兄。

turns.en: Is there any argument against this? Has anybody tried to debate this? Has anybody tried to say that this is nonsense, this is a conspiracy? Has anybody made any sort of a rational argument?
turns.zh: 对此有任何反对意见吗？有人试图辩论过吗？有人试图说这是胡说八道，是阴谋论吗？有人提出过任何理性的论点吗？

turns.en: The left actually interestingly does not want to pick up much on this argument because the more attention you look, the more you look at it, the more obviously it is true. Because you just say, well, are the numbers correct? Have there really been this many illegals that have been imported into swing states? Yes. They haven't just walked across the border. They've been flowing in, flowing in in airplanes. Yeah, using a shipping app. Yes. The app always existed, but it used to be for people coming over here like shipping with goods, so they could track you while you're in America, so you could legally be here. They know where you are. And then they changed it to allow that app to schedule illegal aliens to come across the border. Yes. Asylum seekers, come on in. Yes. Oh, you have an app. People are literally being flown in, yeah, to the swing states. And the reason I think the left doesn't want to push back on this is because the more attention this gets, the more people realize it is true. It is true. That's why they don't. They're just pretending. They're pretending I'm not saying anything. But I'm like, yo, they're literally flying vast numbers of illegals who are then beholden to the Democrats. And sometimes I get a bit of pushback from people saying, well, you know, these illegals don't have the same social values as the Democrat Party because they're more socially conservative. I'm like, yeah, but that's not the point. If you look at Maslow's hierarchy of needs, their primary thing is staying in the country and getting their friends and family in. And then the Democrats give them all these benefits, like tons of benefits, more benefits than if you were citizens. Literally, yeah. So they are beholden to the Democrats for all these benefits. They want to get their friends and family in, which the Democrats support and the Republicans don't. So they vote Democrat. And you can look empirically at California and say, did they vote Republican or Democrat in California? Oh, they voted Democrat. Well, Reagan gave them amnesty in the 1980s, and that changed the state basically, except for Arnold. Changed the state entirely blue. Yes. And Arnold was an exception because he was like a socially liberal famous guy, yeah, and didn't really impose any radical restrictions on any of the people that were going to vote Democrat in the first place. The whole thing is just bizarre to watch play out because it just seems like this can't actually be what's happening.
turns.zh: 有趣的是，左派实际上不想过多讨论这个论点，因为你越关注它，越审视它，它就越明显是真实的。因为你只需问，数字正确吗？真的有这么多非法移民被运进摇摆州吗？是的。他们不只是走过边境。他们一直在涌入，乘坐飞机涌入。是的，用一款运输应用程序。是的。这个应用程序一直存在，但以前是用来给来这里的人用的，比如运输货物，这样他们可以在你在美国时追踪你，让你合法地待在这里。他们知道你在哪里。然后他们改变了它，允许这个应用程序安排非法移民穿越边境。是的。寻求庇护者，进来吧。是的。哦，你有个应用程序。人们真的被空运进来，是的，到摇摆州。我认为左派不想反驳的原因是，这件事越受关注，越多人意识到它是真的。这是真的。这就是为什么他们不反驳。他们只是在假装。他们假装我什么都没说。但我说，嘿，他们真的在空运大量非法移民，这些人随后就受制于民主党。有时我会遇到一些人的反驳，他们说，这些非法移民没有民主党的社会价值观，因为他们更社会保守。我说，是的，但这不是重点。如果你看马斯洛需求层次，他们的首要任务是留在美国，并把朋友和家人弄进来。然后民主党给他们所有这些福利，大量的福利，比公民还多。真的，是的。所以他们因为所有这些福利而受制于民主党。他们想把朋友和家人弄进来，民主党支持，共和党反对。所以他们投民主党。你可以凭经验看看加州，他们在加州投共和党还是民主党？哦，他们投了民主党。里根在 1980 年代给了他们大赦，那基本上改变了这个州，除了阿诺德。把整个州变成了蓝色。是的。阿诺德是个例外，因为他是个社会自由派的名人，而且没有对本来就会投民主党的人施加任何激进的限制。整件事看起来很奇怪，因为感觉这不可能是真的。

turns.en: Did you see my conversation with Friedman about it? He was completely in denial about it. I don't think there's that level of organization. What are you talking about? Exactly. Because you can break it down. Are any of these numbers wrong? Because we got these numbers from Homeland Security, government.gov. Okay, right. So we got it from the .gov website. Has the government reported these numbers incorrectly? No, they have not. Those numbers, if anything, are low. So okay, so they have in fact flown vast numbers of illegals to swing states, bypassing the border entirely. And that is factually true. Then you say, well, what is their probable voting pattern? Overwhelmingly Democrat. Into swing states. And then, well, but do the Democrats actually want to fast-track them for citizenship? Oh, yes. You can see Chuck Schumer on TV at a rally this year saying he wants to fast-track and make all 11 million or however many, I believe his quote was, citizens as soon as possible. So the goal is to fast-track citizenship as quickly as possible so they can vote. Whether one thinks it's cheating or not, it won't matter because they will be fully able to vote. And for people on the left, this is actually happening. I invite people to rebut this and show me where I am wrong. Please do so. No, they can't. They can't. They can't because it's true.
turns.zh: 你看到我和弗里德曼的对话了吗？他完全否认这一点。我不认为有那种组织水平。你在说什么？没错。因为你可以分解来看。这些数字有错吗？因为我们从国土安全部、政府网站得到这些数字。好的，没错。所以我们从 .gov 网站得到这些数字。政府报告这些数字有误吗？不，没有。这些数字，如果说有什么，是偏低的。所以，好的，他们确实空运了大量非法移民到摇摆州，完全绕过了边境。这是事实。然后你说，他们可能的投票模式是什么？压倒性地投民主党。进入摇摆州。然后，但民主党真的想让他们快速获得公民身份吗？哦，是的。你可以看到查克·舒默今年在一次集会上在电视上说，他想快速处理，让所有 1100 万或多少，我相信他的原话是，尽快成为公民。所以目标是尽快加快公民身份，这样他们就能投票。无论有人认为这是否作弊，都不重要，因为他们将完全能够投票。对于左派的人来说，这确实在发生。我邀请人们反驳这一点，并指出我哪里错了。请这样做。不，他们不能。他们不能。他们不能，因为这是真的。

turns.en: Well, what's scary to me is that there are people on the left, like people that were Bernie Sanders supporters, for example. Screwed with, like talk about undermining democracy. Bernie should have won the nomination, and they stole it from him and gave it to Hillary. Exactly. Exactly. That's what I was going to bring up. They control the primary process. Yes, exactly. So if you have a Democratic primary, it's not democratic. We just saw that. We saw it with Bernie. We saw it with Kamala. Like a week before Biden was summarily fired, he was posting that he's in it for the long term. He's going. Yeah, yeah. He's not giving up. Next thing you know, Sunday afternoon, they're posting on X that he's resigned from the race. Which is insane.
turns.zh: 嗯，让我害怕的是，左派中有些人，比如曾是伯尼·桑德斯支持者的人。搞砸了，就像谈论破坏民主一样。伯尼本应赢得提名，但他们从他那里偷走了，给了希拉里。没错。没错。这正是我想提的。他们控制了初选过程。是的，没错。所以如果你有一个民主党初选，它并不民主。我们刚刚看到了。我们在伯尼身上看到了。我们在卡马拉身上看到了。就在拜登被突然解雇前一周，他还在发帖说他长期参与。他继续。是的，是的。他不会放弃。接下来，周日下午，他们在 X 上发帖说他退出了竞选。这太疯狂了。

ts.sec: Biden's mental state and who's running the country

turns.en: His staff didn't even know; they're reading it on the X platform. That's how they learned about it. What do you think happened there? How did they do that? Because clearly, he's just not in charge. Obviously, they could have used the 25th Amendment, fake president, but they would have to admit that there was a certain period of time where they knew that he was mentally compromised. Yes, and so they made this decision not to do that.
turns.zh: 他的幕僚甚至都不知道，他们是在 X 平台上看到的。他们就是这样得知的。你觉得发生了什么？他们是怎么做到的？因为很明显，他根本不在掌控之中。显然，他们本可以动用第 25 修正案，弄个假总统，但他们必须承认，有一段时间他们知道他精神上出了问题。是的，所以他们决定不这么做。

turns.en: Well, the weird thing is that the president's supposed to be the boss, right? And yet he's obviously not the boss. So who's running the country? If she's busy campaigning, she's so busy she can't do anything except sit life. She did that. She's so busy she's constantly campaigning. How could you be paying attention to international relations? Yeah, how could you be paying attention to the economy? How could you be paying attention to any of those things? How do you have the time? You can't. Yeah, I mean, Biden being the president's supposed to be the CEO, the chief guy, he was commander-in-chief. But it just obviously that Biden was not; he was just a puppet. And when the various puppet masters decided that the puppet was no longer useful, they just tossed out the puppet and then got a new puppet with Kamala. I mean, Kamala can't even talk.
turns.zh: 嗯，奇怪的是，总统应该是老板，对吧？但他显然不是老板。那谁在管理国家？如果她忙着竞选，她忙得除了坐等什么也做不了。她确实那样做了。她忙得一直在竞选。你怎么可能关注国际关系？是啊，你怎么可能关注经济？你怎么可能关注那些事情？你怎么有时间？你做不到。是的，我的意思是，拜登作为总统应该是 CEO，是老大，他是三军统帅。但很明显拜登不是；他只是一个傀儡。当各种傀儡大师决定这个傀儡不再有用时，他们就把傀儡扔了，然后换了一个新傀儡，就是卡玛拉。我是说，卡玛拉连话都说不清楚。

turns.en: Now you invited her on your show. I think the most damage that could possibly be done to a campaign is going on your show and seeing what she says in hours two and three. Two and three is when things get spicy. I'm like, oh my God, you can hide for 20 minutes. Melt, you can hide for 20 minutes. Exactly, yeah. I mean, you can just regurgitate talking points for half an hour, maybe an hour, just where she's just saying non sequiturs. But eventually she just runs out of even the non sequiturs.
turns.zh: 现在你邀请她上你的节目。我认为对一个竞选活动可能造成的最大损害就是上你的节目，看看她在第二、第三个小时说了什么。第二、第三个小时事情就变得有趣了。我就想，天哪，你可以躲 20 分钟。融化，你可以躲 20 分钟。没错，是的。我的意思是，你可以重复半小时、也许一小时的谈话要点，她说的都是些不连贯的话。但最终她连不连贯的话都说不出来了。

turns.en: Well, they wanted to limit it to an hour, exactly that's why. But I was thinking of doing it initially before Trump came here. First of all, when they found out that there was a rumor, I never announced that Trump was coming. What I was going to do is just release it in my... the way I like to do things, I don't like to tell anybody who's coming on. It'll get big no matter what. If Trump was on, it would have been huge. I'm like, just put it out there, people go crazy. But he apparently, or someone from his organization, some loose lips, and then it got out. And so she contacted my management company, and her campaign contacted us and said, would Joe have Rana? I said yes. And they said she wants you to fly to where she is, and she's only willing to do 45 minutes, only 40. I mean, that's... and I was like, I don't know. So I thought about doing it. I'm like, maybe I can get a sense, maybe I could convince her, maybe I could coax her into doing more time. I just wanted to talk to her. I don't give a what we talk about, we talk about recipes. I don't give a... exactly, just talk to you. Just the things like you just can't... like you can't just output non sequiturs for three hours, right? Um, so but for 45 minutes, you could do... uh, I thought maybe for 45 minutes I could get something out of it. But then when Trump came and did the 3 hours, I was like, you know what, it has to be like this. This is the only... to be fair, it's got to be like three hours, and it should be in this room because this room has like a history of people. Yeah, it's got good vibes.
turns.zh: 嗯，他们想限制在一小时，正是这个原因。但我最初考虑在特朗普来之前就做。首先，当他们发现有传言时，我从未宣布特朗普要来。我打算做的是直接发布……我喜欢的方式是，我不告诉任何人谁要来。无论如何都会火。如果特朗普上了，那会非常轰动。我就想，直接放出去，人们会疯狂的。但他显然，或者他组织里的某个人，嘴不严，消息就泄露了。于是她联系了我的管理公司，她的竞选团队联系我们说，乔愿意让拉娜上吗？我说愿意。他们说她想让你飞到她那里，而且她只愿意做 45 分钟，只有 40 分钟。我是说，那……我就想，我不知道。所以我考虑过做。我想，也许我能了解一下，也许我能说服她，也许我能哄她多花点时间。我只是想和她谈谈。我不在乎我们谈什么，我们谈食谱都行。我不在乎……没错，就是和你聊聊。只是有些事你做不到……你不能连续三个小时只说不连贯的话，对吧？嗯，所以 45 分钟的话，你可以……呃，我想也许 45 分钟我能从中得到点什么。但后来特朗普来了，做了三个小时，我就想，你知道，必须这样。这是唯一……公平地说，必须三个小时，而且应该在这个房间，因为这个房间有历史感。是啊，它有好氛围。

turns.en: It's always fascinating to me how one company can dominate a market. Apple's dominated the cell phone market largely by making the best product, but also YouTube has dominated the video market. That one's the most bizarre to me because it seems like there should be a ton of options. It seems like it's not that difficult to pull off, but nothing ever took hold other than X.
turns.zh: 我一直觉得一家公司能主导一个市场很有意思。苹果主导手机市场很大程度上是因为做出了最好的产品，但 YouTube 也主导了视频市场。那个我觉得最奇怪，因为按理说应该有很多选择。看起来也不是很难做到，但除了 X 之外没有其他平台真正站稳脚跟。

turns.en: I think one of the big changes was when Tucker Carlson decided to do his show from X straight out of Fox. Then people realized, "Oh, you can watch full videos on X the same exact way you could watch them on YouTube." It's not as simple in terms of the algorithm, but it'll get better. And it is now possible to watch X videos on your big TV. You can actually download the X app on your TV and watch it on your TV.
turns.zh: 我觉得一个重大变化是 Tucker Carlson 决定从 Fox 直接转到 X 做他的节目。然后人们意识到：“哦，你可以在 X 上看完整视频，就跟在 YouTube 上看一样。”算法方面还不够简单，但会改进的。现在也可以在电视上看 X 视频了。你可以在电视上下载 X 应用，然后在电视上看。

turns.en: Yes. So we'll make it so that you can watch X videos on a big TV. It doesn't have to be on your phone or your iPad or something like that.
turns.zh: 可以。所以我们会让你能在电视上看 X 视频，不一定非要在手机或 iPad 上看。

turns.en: If it wasn't for you buying Twitter and changing it, I don't think we would be where we are right now. I think it was a pivotal moment. Historically, when people look back, it's going to be a pivotal moment in this very bizarre fight for the freedom of information.
turns.zh: 如果不是你收购并改变了 Twitter，我认为我们不会走到今天这一步。我认为这是一个关键时刻。从历史角度看，当人们回顾时，这将是这场非常离奇的信息自由斗争中的一个关键时刻。

turns.en: Well, at the time I said, look, I think this is existential to the United States. It's existential to democracy. Because if you don't have freedom of speech, you don't have democracy. If people cannot make an informed vote because they're just being fed propaganda, and there's no freedom of speech, democracy is an illusion. So freedom of speech is the bedrock of democracy. That's why freedom of speech is the First Amendment. Once you lose freedom of speech, you lose democracy, game over. That's why I bought Twitter. It seems so simple. It seems so clear that everyone should agree to that, on the left or on the right. Imagine the Bush Administration during the Iraq War, if they had complete control of propaganda and of dissent online. You don't want that. No one wants that. No one from the left would want that. We shouldn't want it from the left either.
turns.zh: 嗯，我当时说过，我认为这对美国来说是生死攸关的，对民主来说是生死攸关的。因为如果没有言论自由，就没有民主。如果人们无法做出明智的投票，因为他们只是被灌输宣传，而没有言论自由，民主就是幻觉。所以言论自由是民主的基石。这就是为什么言论自由是第一修正案。一旦失去言论自由，你就失去了民主，游戏结束。这就是我收购 Twitter 的原因。这看起来很简单。这看起来很清楚，每个人都应该同意这一点，无论左派还是右派。想象一下伊拉克战争期间的小布什政府，如果他们完全控制了宣传和网络异议。你不想要那样。没有人想要那样。左派也不会想要那样。我们也不应该从左派的角度想要那样。

turns.en: There's also the legacy media, the mainstream media. It used to be much more balanced. Political donations over time, Republican versus Democrat, used to be like two-thirds Democrat, one-third Republican in terms of journalists making donations. Now it's like 95% Democrat. So the legacy media is not balanced at all. They're just a mouthpiece for the Democratic Party. You can see that in how consistent their headlines are. turns.zh: 很久以前，总统……这跟选举年有什么关系？但这就是惩罚，就像你越界了，你支持了另一个人。问题是你永远接不到电影或演唱会的邀约了，这很疯狂。我的意思是，过去我们允许人们是共和党人，同时还是电影明星，比如克林特·伊斯特伍德、里根。在奥巴马执政期间，克林特·伊斯特伍德是个直言不讳的共和党人，但他仍然是巨星，人们会说“啊，那是克林特”，他被允许有不同的意见。但现在不行了。特朗普上台后，他成了焦点，所有逻辑都被抛到窗外，只剩下“特朗普是坏人，你必须攻击特朗普，右翼都是坏人，基督徒都是坏人”。这很奇怪。所以我要再说一遍，如果特朗普不赢，这就是最后一次选举了。你说得对。很多人正在觉醒，包括一些终身民主党人，比如比尔·阿曼、图尔西·加巴德，他们转投了共和党。很多人一辈子都是左翼，现在意识到“我不能再这样了”。你和我以前也是民主党人。这太疯狂了。我认为我们想要的东西很基本：个人自由、机会，让美国保持自由和机遇之地。我们最大化个人自由，政府不能闯进你家杀死你的宠物。成功取决于努力和才能，而不是种族、宗教、性别。这是基本的东西。你把 DEI 的缩写改成了什么？DIEE？DIE？因为多样性、包容性和公平是 DEI，但你改成了奉献、卓越……是的，我们想要美国成为机遇之地，意味着成功取决于努力和技能。这很激进。现在最优秀的人会让你变成右翼。好吧，我不在乎。没有安全的边界就不是真正的国家，只是虚假的国家。我们的城市不安全、肮脏。我妈妈告诉我，她现在已经很“红药丸”了，但让你快速觉醒的是朋友在纽约街头被袭击。今年我们三个朋友在纽约街头走路时被袭击了。没有人被捕，什么都没有发生。警察的士气严重低落。如果你是一名警察，逮捕一个暴力分子是在冒生命危险，因为他们可能会试图杀死你。但如果你知道逮捕后他们会被地方检察官立即释放——在纽约，阿尔文·布拉格不起诉人——那警察为什么要冒生命危险去逮捕一个知道会被立即释放的人？这毫无意义。就像小丑在掌权，罪犯逍遥法外，公民却被逮捕。这就是为什么我还在震惊于那个松鼠事件。他们持枪强迫一个人待在屋外，然后抓走他的宠物并杀死它们。与此同时，暴力重罪犯在纽约街头自由游荡。这就像小丑的世界。守法的公民被逮捕，罪犯却自由了。他们竟然有资源去杀别人的松鼠，而他们还有那么多犯罪要处理。
