turns.zh: 这个政府效率机构的想法……你想叫它什么？我觉得最搞笑的名字是“狗狗部”，政府效率部。想法很简单：我们有一个令人窒息的庞大联邦官僚机构，政府开支正在让国家破产。国债利息支出已经超过了国防部预算，国防预算每年约一万亿美元，而国债利息现在更高，而且每月都在增长。我们正走在破产的路上，就像超支的波斯特伍德一样。更糟的是，我们在所有这些政府机构上花钱。我问了 AI 有多少个政府机构，政府自己都不确定。大约 450 个，取决于你怎么定义“机构”。联邦层面的机构数量几乎是美国建国年数的两倍，我们大约每年创建两个机构。这太疯狂了。我怀疑有没有一个人能说出所有 450 个联邦机构的名字。可能没有，但几乎没人能做到。我打赌大多数人连 100 个都说不出来。所以我们有庞大、令人窒息的联邦官僚机构，每年都在膨胀。最终，一切都会变得非法，你什么都做不成。那么能做什么呢？显然总统有很大的权力，但如何……

turns.en: Much power and what can be done in terms of like eliminating agencies, eliminating waste, eliminating... yeah.
turns.zh: 很多权力，以及像裁撤机构、消除浪费、消除……嗯，这方面能做什么？

turns.en: Well, so if Congress has created an agency, then often if you look at the law, the law is pretty simple. Like the agency has a very simple task, but then that agency over time vastly increases its authority and starts doing things that were never authorized by Congress. That's happened with pretty much every agency. So yeah, you'd have to still keep an agency, you'd have to match the law, but you can tailor the agencies to be much smaller and say, 'You got to stick to what Congress authorized instead of all this other stuff you're doing,' which I think makes sense.
turns.zh: 嗯，如果国会设立了一个机构，通常法律本身很简单——该机构的任务很明确，但随着时间的推移，它会大幅扩张自己的权力，开始做国会从未授权的事情。几乎所有机构都这样。所以，是的，你仍然需要保留机构，必须遵守法律，但你可以把机构精简到更小，并说：“你们必须只做国会授权的事，而不是你们现在做的这些其他事情。”我觉得这很合理。

turns.en: So is the other stuff they're doing just essentially bureaucracy run amok? Or they just create jobs and create things to do and create a meaning for their existence?
turns.zh: 所以他们做的其他事情本质上就是官僚主义失控？还是他们只是为了创造岗位、找事做、为自己的存在找意义？

turns.en: Yeah, it's like a tumor, it's just going to keep growing. Jesus Christ. And it's so... I mean, for SpaceX, Starship was sitting on the pad, the rocket, with the giant rocket. We could build the rocket faster than they could process the paperwork to approve the launch. So we were sitting there for two months.
turns.zh: 是的，就像肿瘤一样，它会不断增长。天哪。而且……我是说，对 SpaceX 来说，星舰就停在发射台上，那个巨大的火箭。我们造火箭的速度比他们审批发射文件的速度还快。所以我们干等了两个月。

turns.en: But do you think that they're doing that on purpose to you?
turns.zh: 但你觉得他们是故意针对你吗？

turns.en: I can't... I mean, maybe a little, but that was also not cool. Yeah. But another way to think of it is the amount of paperwork is going to go roughly with the square of the number of agencies involved, because they all have to meet with each other. So let's say in a best-case situation, if you're dealing with one agency, that's one thing. But if you've got to deal with five agencies, and the agencies have to meet with each other, now you've got like 25 different meeting configurations that have to take place. And it just... everything just gets hardened of the arteries. You just can't make progress. This is why we can't build high-speed rail in America. It's basically illegal, right?
turns.zh: 我不能……我是说，可能有一点，但那也不酷。嗯。但换个角度看，文书工作量大致与涉及机构数量的平方成正比，因为它们之间都要互相开会。假设最理想的情况，如果你只跟一个机构打交道，那是一回事。但如果你要跟五个机构打交道，而且这些机构之间还得互相开会，那就有 25 种不同的会议组合需要安排。结果就是……一切都像动脉硬化一样，你根本没法推进。这就是为什么美国建不了高铁。基本上就是非法的，对吧？

turns.en: So this has been the argument: we need regulation because we need to protect the environment, we need to protect people, we need to make sure the rule of law is followed. So we need a certain amount of regulation, but overregulation is a giant problem. That's a big issue in California. It's a huge issue anywhere where bureaucracy has run amok. They make it very difficult to get anything done.
turns.zh: 所以一直以来的论点就是：我们需要监管，因为要保护环境、保护人民、确保法治得到遵守。所以我们需要一定程度的监管，但过度监管是个大问题。这在加州是个大问题，在任何官僚主义失控的地方都是个大问题。他们让任何事情都很难办成。

turns.en: Yes. I mean, what happens is every year there are more rules and regulations created. And in the past, what has served as a cleansing function for rules and regulations is war, because like, 'Well, we're going to lose if we don't kind of clear the decks.' But we haven't really had an existential threat of war in the US. We've had prosperity for a long time, which has resulted in a massive buildup of rules and regulations every year, to the point where, like I said, everything's illegal. And it's not like any one regulation is the problem. It's like a giant being tied down by a million little strings. It's not like any one string is the problem, but you got a million of them. So we've got to clear the decks here. And I'm not saying we shouldn't have regulators. I'm just saying we've gone way too far. Once you think of regulators like referees on a field, a sports field, you don't want to have no refs. You want to have some number of refs, but you don't want to have way more refs than players, right? You don't want to be like, well, the running back couldn't complete the run because there were too many regulators in the way, because the football field's full of regulators. Yeah, you know, you can't even play the game right. That's the issue we've got right now.
turns.zh: 是的。我的意思是，每年都会有更多的规则和条例被制定出来。过去，战争起到了清理规则和条例的作用，因为“如果我们不清空甲板，我们就会输”。但美国并没有真正面临战争的生存威胁。我们长期繁荣，这导致每年规则和条例大量堆积，到了我说的一切都非法的地步。而且并不是某一条规定有问题。就像一个巨人被一百万根细绳绑住。不是哪一根绳子的问题，而是你有一百万根。所以我们得清空甲板。我不是说我们不应该有监管者。我只是说我们走得太远了。把监管者想象成球场上的裁判，体育场上的裁判。你不想没有裁判。你想要一定数量的裁判，但你不想要裁判比球员还多，对吧？你不想看到跑卫没法完成跑动，因为路上有太多监管者，因为足球场上全是裁判。是的，你甚至没法正常比赛。这就是我们现在的问题。

turns.en: Well, that's a great analogy. Yeah, I can imagine a football field that's filled with referees. The football field's filled with refs. You can't even run past them.
turns.zh: 嗯，这个比喻很好。是的，我能想象一个满是裁判的足球场。足球场上全是裁判，你甚至没法从他们身边跑过去。

turns.en: Yeah, yeah.
turns.zh: 是的，是的。

turns.en: I've seen criticism of this idea of you coming up with this department of like firing a bunch of people and what would happen and how would that work. But the criticism doesn't make any sense to me because if you measurably, if you can prove that there's a lot of wasted time and resources, which I think is pretty easy to do, and if you could say that this is not the most efficient... Like the most efficient businesses are generally private businesses or a company because they kind of have to be in order to stay profitable. Yeah, the government doesn't have to be profitable. They don't have to be efficient. They don't have competition. So if you're making cars and your cars break down and they suck, and someone makes cars and the cars are better, they're going to succeed. So this is the free market. The government doesn't have this problem. When they're in charge of certain things that could probably be better served by the public, by the private sector.
turns.zh: 我看到过对你这个想法的批评，就是你成立一个部门来解雇一大批人，会发生什么，怎么运作。但我觉得这些批评毫无道理，因为如果你能可衡量地证明存在大量时间和资源浪费——我认为这很容易做到——而且你能说这不是最高效的……最高效的企业通常是私营企业或公司，因为它们必须这样才能保持盈利。是的，政府不需要盈利。他们不需要高效。他们没有竞争。所以如果你造车，你的车老是坏、很烂，而别人造的车更好，他们就会成功。这就是自由市场。政府没有这个问题。当他们负责某些事情时，这些事可能由公共部门或私营部门来服务会更好。

turns.en: Yeah, absolutely. Well, look, I just think we've gotten far too many government agencies. The federal bureaucracy has gotten out of hand. And we just need to pare it down to a sensible level. And if it turns out that there's some regulation or agency that was doing something useful, we can put it right back. No problem. Like, 'Oh, that regulation was important.' No problem, we put it right back. Right? As long as we can look at it logically and objectively.
turns.zh: 是的，绝对如此。嗯，听着，我只是觉得我们的政府机构太多了。联邦官僚机构已经失控了。我们只需要把它削减到一个合理的水平。如果发现某些规定或机构确实在做有用的事，我们可以马上恢复。没问题。就像“哦，那条规定很重要。”没问题，我们马上恢复。对吧？只要我们能逻辑地、客观地看待它。

turns.en: And you were also floating around the idea of offering a large severance to the people that you're going to have removed. Yeah, like a couple years or something like that. Is that what you're saying?
turns.zh: 你还提出过给将被解雇的人提供一大笔遣散费的想法。比如两年的工资之类的。你是这个意思吗？

turns.en: Yeah, I mean, these are again just ideas. But the point is not that people suffer economic hardship. The point is just that they're better off doing more productive things in the economy, and it would be better if they did these other more productive things, and we didn't have this vast federal bureaucracy. So I was like, 'Hmm, maybe a couple years of pay would be good.' And then they could take a vacation, they could take another job and get double pay. It's not going to create an economic crisis. I think it's actually going to be really good, because people can move to where they're making products and services that are more useful to their fellow human beings.
turns.zh: 是的，我的意思是，这些又只是想法。但重点不是让人们遭受经济困难。重点是他们在经济中做更有生产力的事情会更好，如果他们做这些更有生产力的事情，而我们没有这个庞大的联邦官僚机构，那会更好。所以我想，“嗯，也许两年的工资不错。”然后他们可以度个假，可以再找份工作拿双份工资。这不会造成经济危机。我认为实际上会很好，因为人们可以转向生产对同胞更有用的产品和服务。

turns.en: The problem is if someone has like a 25-30 year career of being institutionalized, you're essentially like a part of the government system. You've sort of programmed your life and your career to be a part of this bureaucratic system, and then you're like, 'Nope, you have to go out and compete in the free market.' You're like, 'Ah, that's scary to people.' But you have to be valuable. You have to actually be valuable.
turns.zh: 问题是，如果一个人有 25 到 30 年的体制内职业生涯，他本质上就是政府系统的一部分。他的人生和职业都已经被编程为这个官僚系统的一部分，然后你说，“不，你必须出去在自由市场竞争。”人们会觉得“啊，这很可怕。”但你必须要有价值。你必须真正有价值。

turns.en: Yeah, yeah. I mean, let's look...
turns.zh: 是的，是的。我是说，让我们看看……

turns.en: At like you know whatever the government pension and stuff, they're not going to be in tough. I think they'll be in good financial shape. How are you going to have the time to oversee all this?
turns.zh: 比如政府养老金之类的，它们不会陷入困境。我认为它们财务状况会很好。你打算怎么有时间来监督这一切？

turns.en: Well, I'm pretty good at improving efficiency. I mean, I would say so. But still, this seems like a giant undertaking. Yeah, I'll probably need to beef up security. Oh yeah, yeah, for sure. But you know, like I said, no one's going to experience economic hardship. They'll be fine. And people do find other roles. I mean, you can look at when East Germany and West Germany got back together. Everyone was basically working for the government in East Germany, and it was really inefficient. Their economic output in East Germany was like a quarter of what it was in West Germany because everyone was working for the government. Government is fundamentally inefficient. The best example is probably North and South Korea. People are starving in North Korea, and South Korea is incredibly prosperous. Same people, just different operating system. So you just want to move people from less productive things to more productive things. You can also consider the other direction: if we moved a whole bunch of people from the private sector making goods and services into the government as regulators, they stop making those goods and services. The stuff they were making is no longer available, and now they're just regulators. Is that a good thing? That's not a good thing.
turns.zh: 嗯，我很擅长提高效率。我是说，我确实这么认为。但这仍然是个巨大的工程。是的，我可能需要加强安保。哦，是的，当然。但你知道，就像我说的，没有人会经历经济困难。他们会没事的。而且人们确实会找到其他角色。我的意思是，你可以看看东德和西德统一的时候。东德基本上所有人都在为政府工作，效率非常低。东德的经济产出只有西德的四分之一，因为所有人都在为政府工作。政府从根本上就是低效的。最好的例子可能是朝鲜和韩国。朝鲜人民在挨饿，而韩国非常繁荣。同样的人，只是不同的操作系统。所以你只是想把人们从低生产力的事情转移到高生产力的事情上。你也可以考虑另一个方向：如果我们把一大批从事商品和服务生产的私营部门人员调到政府当监管者，他们就不再生产那些商品和服务了。他们生产的东西不再可用，而他们现在只是监管者。这是好事吗？这不是好事。

turns.en: Doesn't sound good. No, it's not good. Doesn't sound like there's a real market for it. You're creating jobs that don't necessarily need to be there. They're all these fake jobs basically. And that doesn't make sense.
turns.zh: 听起来不好。不，这不好。听起来没有真正的市场需求。你在创造一些不一定需要存在的岗位。基本上都是些假工作。这说不通。

turns.en: So look, we've got to do this because the country's going bankrupt. If we don't take action, the dollar will be worth nothing. The interest payments, which are already 23% of all government income — call it income taxes, tariffs, and everything — are just going to pay interest right now, and that number is continually rising. If we don't do something, the entire government budget will be paying interest. There won't be money for anything. No money for Social Security, no money for Medicare, nothing. That's where we're headed. That's what bankruptcy means.
turns.zh: 所以听着，我们必须这么做，因为国家要破产了。如果我们不采取行动，美元将一文不值。利息支付已经占到政府全部收入的 23%——包括所得税、关税等等——现在这些钱都用来付利息了，而且这个数字还在不断上升。如果我们不做点什么，整个政府预算都将用来支付利息。就没有钱做任何事了。没有钱用于社会保障，没有钱用于医疗保险，什么都没有。这就是我们的方向。这就是破产的意思。

turns.en: That's such an insane concept. Yes. Hello, wake up, wake up. And if somebody can show me the math, show me how this works, I'd love to hear it. But I'm looking at the numbers here, and I'm like if we don't do something, America's toast. There won't be money for anything.
turns.zh: 这概念太疯狂了。是的。喂，醒醒，醒醒。如果有人能给我算笔账，告诉我这怎么行得通，我很乐意听听。但我看着这些数字，我觉得如果我们不做点什么，美国就完蛋了。什么钱都不会有。

turns.en: Trump likes to talk a lot about tariffs. What are your thoughts on tariffs? I know that's very controversial. Even economists disagree. Some agree, some think it's a good idea, some think it's a terrible idea. What do you think?
turns.zh: 特朗普很喜欢谈论关税。你对关税有什么看法？我知道这很有争议。连经济学家都意见不一。有些人同意，有些人认为这是个好主意，有些人认为这是个糟糕的主意。你怎么看？

turns.en: I think you need to be careful with tariffs. I deal a lot with supply chain issues. The global automotive supply chain for Tesla, for example, is incredibly complex. So when there are sudden changes in tariffs, then you have a factory somewhere else making a part that goes into the car. Now suddenly that part is twice as expensive, and it messes everything up. So you want tariffs to be predictable, so that companies can adjust their supply chain. I think companies are more than happy to increase manufacturing in America. It's just that you can't do it instantly. If you put up giant tariffs immediately and don't give companies a chance to build factories in America — because you have to move atoms, build a building, install equipment, train people — that doesn't happen instantly. So you want to have a ramp for tariffs so that companies can adjust, build the factories, train the people, and get the equipment in place. Otherwise, you just shock the system, and it breaks or bad things happen. So I'm against sudden giant tariffs because it's an impossible response if you have to move a thousand tons of equipment, or collectively millions of tons. You can't do that overnight. It's literally impossible. So I think we want to be thoughtful about tariffs and give companies a ramp. I do generally agree that America should do more manufacturing. I'm a big manufacturing guy. I love manufacturing. So I've spent a lot of time in the factory.
turns.zh: 我认为你需要谨慎对待关税。我经常处理供应链问题。例如，特斯拉的全球汽车供应链极其复杂。所以当关税突然变化时，你在其他地方有一个工厂生产汽车零件。现在那个零件突然贵了一倍，就把一切都搞乱了。所以你希望关税是可预测的，这样公司就能调整他们的供应链。我认为公司非常乐意增加在美国的制造。只是你不能瞬间完成。如果你立即征收高额关税，而不给公司机会在美国建厂——因为你必须移动原子，建造建筑，安装设备，培训人员——这不会瞬间发生。所以你需要一个关税的缓冲期，让公司能够调整，建厂，培训人员，并安装好设备。否则，你只是冲击了系统，它就会崩溃或发生坏事。所以我反对突然征收高额关税，因为如果你要移动一千吨设备，或者总共几百万吨设备，这是不可能应对的。你不可能一夜之间做到。这根本不可能。所以我认为我们应该对关税深思熟虑，并给公司一个缓冲期。我总体上同意美国应该做更多的制造。我是一个制造迷。我喜欢制造。所以我花了很多时间在工厂里。

turns.en: We've talked openly about the difficulties of manufacturing and how complicated it is. Most people aren't really aware of something as complex as building a Tesla. Manufacturing is super hard and complicated. A lot of people have never been in a factory or don't know how difficult it is to make things. For a lot of people, they think ketchup comes from the store. The store just has it. For a lot of people who've been in academia or socialist communist types, they've never actually made anything. They operate on the premise that there's this magical horn of plenty that just outputs goods and services, and if someone has more goods and services than someone else, it's because they took more from this magical horn of plenty. I'm like, guys, there's no magical horn of plenty. There's no cornucopia. Goods and services come from people working collectively, doing a lot of hard work to produce the goods and services that you like and need. But we've become very accustomed to these things happening overseas. America is still the second biggest manufacturer in the world, so we still make a lot of stuff, but we could make more. Probably should make more. I think we should value manufacturing a lot more in the United States than we currently do.
turns.zh: 我们公开谈论过制造的困难以及它有多复杂。大多数人并不真正了解像制造一辆特斯拉这样复杂的事情。制造超级难，超级复杂。很多人从未进过工厂，或者不知道制造东西有多难。对很多人来说，他们以为番茄酱是从商店里来的。商店里就有。对于很多在学术界或社会主义共产主义类型的人来说，他们从未真正制造过任何东西。他们基于一个前提运作，那就是存在一个神奇的大聚宝盆，源源不断地输出商品和服务，如果有人比其他人拥有更多的商品和服务，那是因为他们从这个神奇的大聚宝盆里拿走了更多。我想说，伙计们，没有神奇的大聚宝盆。没有丰饶之角。商品和服务来自人们集体工作，付出大量辛勤劳动，生产出你喜欢和需要的商品和服务。但我们已经非常习惯这些东西在海外生产。美国仍然是世界第二大制造国，所以我们仍然制造很多东西，但我们可以制造更多。可能应该制造更多。我认为我们应该比现在更重视美国的制造业。

turns.en: It would be very nice if we were completely self-sufficient, like medicine. There's a bunch of different things that get manufactured overseas that was a huge problem during COVID because all the shipping was shut down.
turns.zh: 如果我们完全自给自足就好了，比如药品。有很多不同的东西在海外制造，这在新冠期间是个大问题，因为所有的航运都关闭了。

turns.en: I mean, you don't want to say like, so there's a lot of merit to the economics of comparative advantage.
turns.zh: 我的意思是，你不想说，所以比较优势经济学有很多优点。

turns.en: So if you complete self-sufficient, what that means is that you make all the stuff yourself, and even if some other country is really good at making something, you still make it yourself, which means you're going to have the inferior, more costly product domestically, right? Like Soviet Russia, yeah. Like trade improves prosperity. This is important. So you don't actually want to make everything yourself. You can run this thought experiment on a micro scale or small scale, and then expand that and say at what point does the experiment no longer prove to be valid. Let's consider the case of you as an individual. Imagine you have to do everything yourself: you had to farm, you had to grow chickens, if you want eggs, you've got to build your own house, you've got to do your own electrical repair, your own plumbing, everything yourself. That would be impossible. Now let's expand it to 10 people. Now you're going to have some specialization of tasks. Maybe one person can be really good at construction, another person could be good at farming, but it's still not enough. So let's go to 100 people, now let's go to 100 million people, now let's go to a billion people. You still get the economics of specialization, like specialization of labor where people become expert at particular things. It still matters at a billion people or at 8 billion people, which is Earth. So you still want specialization of labor. You do want countries to be really good at a particular thing and make that thing. Also, it encourages innovation if you have competition. If the Germans are making better cars, we have to make better cars. We have to compete with them, which is one of the things that happened during the 80s and 90s. America was making crap cars and Germany was making much better ones.
turns.zh: 所以，如果你完全自给自足，那就意味着你所有东西都自己造，即使别的国家很擅长造某样东西，你仍然自己造，这意味着你国内的产品会更差、更贵，对吧？就像苏联那样。贸易能提升繁荣，这很重要。所以你其实不想什么都自己造。你可以把这个思想实验放在微观或小规模上推演，然后扩大，问这个实验在哪个点上不再成立。考虑你作为个体的情况。想象你必须自己做所有事：你得种地、养鸡，想要鸡蛋就得自己盖房子、自己修电路、自己通水管，所有事都自己干。那是不可能的。现在扩大到 10 个人。你会有一些任务分工。也许一个人擅长建筑，另一个人擅长种地，但 10 个人还是不够。所以扩大到 100 人，再到 1 亿人，再到 10 亿人。你仍然会得到专业化的经济效应，即劳动分工，人们成为特定领域的专家。这在 10 亿人甚至地球上的 80 亿人时仍然成立。所以你仍然需要劳动分工。你确实希望各国擅长某样东西并制造它。此外，竞争能鼓励创新。如果德国人造的车更好，我们就得造更好的车来竞争，这正是 80 年代和 90 年代发生的事。美国造的车很烂，而德国造的好得多。

turns.en: Yeah, exactly. I mean the Japanese car, I mean basically American car industry got really lazy in the 70s and 80s, and then the Japanese and German car companies came in and just cleaned the clock. There was an old joke that is kind of telling. It's a very old joke: why did the Japanese car companies beat the American car companies? Well, in the Japanese car company you had eight people rowing and one person steering, and in the American car company you had eight people steering and one person rowing. If this was a boat race, Japanese boat: eight people rowing, one person steering. American boat: one person rowing, eight people steering. When the American car company loses the race, they fire the rower. That was actually kind of true. One thing is everyone wants to be the boss, and nobody wants to do the work.
turns.zh: 没错。我的意思是，日本汽车，基本上美国汽车工业在 70 年代和 80 年代变得非常懒惰，然后日本和德国汽车公司进来，把他们打得落花流水。有个老笑话很能说明问题：为什么日本汽车公司打败了美国汽车公司？在日本汽车公司，有八个人划船，一个人掌舵；在美国汽车公司，有八个人掌舵，一个人划船。如果这是一场划船比赛，日本船：八个人划船，一个人掌舵。美国船：一个人划船，八个人掌舵。当美国汽车公司输了比赛，他们就把划船的人炒了。这其实有点真实。每个人都想当老板，没人想干活。

turns.en: One thing that a lot of people are concerned about is the potential disruption that's going to come about with automation and AI, that a lot of these jobs, manufacturing jobs, Teamsters, all that stuff is going to be eliminated. What do I mean? You're at the forefront of this, so how do you see this playing out, and what do you think can be done to mitigate a lot of the loss of purpose that a lot of people are going to feel? Loss of income obviously, Universal Basic Income is being floated about, but that seems to me to only be part of the problem. The other big part of the problem is people losing a sense of purpose.
turns.zh: 很多人担心的是自动化和 AI 将带来的颠覆，很多工作，比如制造业、卡车司机等都会被淘汰。你怎么看？你身处这个领域的前沿，所以你觉得这会如何发展？你认为可以做些什么来缓解很多人会感受到的目标感丧失？收入损失显然是个问题，全民基本收入被提出来，但在我看来这只是问题的一部分。另一个大问题是人们失去目标感。

turns.en: Yeah, now we're talking about something which is still pretty far in the future. How far do you think it is? Well, it's probably 15, 20 years. So we've got immediate issues, short-term issues that are 1 to 3 years, medium-term issues like 5 to 10 years, longer-term issues which are maybe 20 years. Longer term, I think there is this question: if you have AI and robotics, how do you find meaning in life if the computer can do everything better than you can and the robot can do everything better than you can? But we're still a long way to go before that. And I do think it's like 80% likely to be a good outcome, maybe 90%. So I think everyone's going to have their own personal robot, like a personal C-3PO or R2-D2. It's going to be essentially just like everyone has their own phone. Everyone will have their own robot buddy. It would be great if it protected you, like if you walked down the street of New York City, have a Terminator with you. But hopefully we avoid that. We don't want this to be the plot of James Cameron. We want more Gene Roddenberry than James Cameron. But it would be fascinating to watch some rich person walk down the street of New York City flanked by two giant Tesla robots. Tesla robots there to protect you from a bad neighborhood. That would be very interesting. You could potentially see that. Restaurants would probably have no robot rules: leave your robot outside, your robot standing by the table. The future's going to be wild. It's going to be really unpredictable. I don't think most people understand the wave that's coming. It's going to completely drown society and change it forever. It's not going to happen overnight, but 20 years from now, I think there will be more humanoid robots than humans. Really? Yes. That's crazy. So like, we have more guns than people in America, we'll have more robots than people in America as well. You have a bunch of old robots nobody wants anymore. In a historical timeline, 20 years in the past is not that big of a deal. From 1900 to 1920, not that big of a deal. 1920 to 1940, kind of a big deal. 1940 to 1960, things start getting weird. 60 to 80, wow, that's a big difference. 80 to 2000, holy, now you have the internet. 2000 to 2020, whoa, this is nuts: propaganda, social media, YouTube, streaming. 20 years from now, like what are we even talking about? It's going to be that much of a shift.
turns.zh: 是的，我们现在谈论的仍然是相当遥远的未来。你觉得有多远？大概 15 到 20 年吧。所以我们有眼前的问题，短期问题（1 到 3 年），中期问题（5 到 10 年），长期问题（大概 20 年）。长期来看，我认为有一个问题：如果有了 AI 和机器人，当电脑和机器人能做所有事并且比你做得更好时，你如何找到生活的意义？但我们离那一步还很远。而且我确实认为有 80% 的可能性是好结果，也许是 90%。所以我认为每个人都会有自己的个人机器人，就像个人 C-3PO 或 R2-D2。这基本上就像每个人都有一部手机。每个人都会有自己的机器人伙伴。如果它能保护你就太好了，比如你走在纽约街头，身边有个终结者。但希望我们能避免那种情况。我们不想让这成为詹姆斯·卡梅隆的电影情节。我们更想要吉恩·罗登伯里的风格，而不是詹姆斯·卡梅隆。但看着某个富人走在纽约街头，两边各有一个巨大的特斯拉机器人，那会很有趣。特斯拉机器人保护你免受坏社区的侵害。那会非常有趣。你可能会看到这种情况。餐厅可能会规定禁止机器人入内：把你的机器人留在外面，让它站在桌边。未来会很疯狂。它会非常不可预测。我不认为大多数人理解即将到来的浪潮。它会彻底淹没社会并永远改变它。这不会一夜之间发生，但 20 年后，我认为人形机器人的数量会超过人类。真的吗？是的。那太疯狂了。就像美国枪支比人多一样，美国机器人也会比人多。你会有很多没人要的旧机器人。在历史时间线上，过去 20 年不算什么大事。从 1900 到 1920 年，没什么大不了的。1920 到 1940 年，算是大事。1940 到 1960 年，事情开始变得奇怪。60 到 80 年，哇，差别很大。80 到 2000 年，天哪，现在有了互联网。2000 到 2020 年，哇，这太疯狂了：宣传、社交媒体、YouTube、流媒体。20 年后，我们甚至会在谈论什么？变化会那么大。

turns.en: Accelerating and we're in the middle of it, so it's very difficult to sort of feel it while it's happening. It kind of just feels like life, and you just get adapted to the changes.
turns.zh: 技术正在加速，而我们正处于其中，所以很难在发生时真切感受到。它只是感觉像生活本身，你逐渐适应了这些变化。

turns.en: Yeah, I mean people's phones at this point are a supercomputer in their pocket, like an article that can answer any questions. People just take it for granted.
turns.zh: 是的，我的意思是，现在的手机就是口袋里的超级计算机，就像一篇能回答任何问题的文章。人们对此习以为常。

turns.en: Yeah, it's normal. Yeah, they get mad if it doesn't work.
turns.zh: 是啊，这很正常。如果它不工作，他们还会生气。

turns.en: Yeah, it's like Louis CK's joke about using your phone on a plane: you're in the sky floating in the air, and now it will work with Starlink too.
turns.zh: 是的，就像路易·C·K 关于在飞机上用手机的笑话：你在空中漂浮，而现在它也能用星链了。

turns.en: What's that? It will work with Starlink? The Starlink connection? It'll be like being on the ground.
turns.zh: 什么？它会用星链？星链连接？就像在地面上一样。

turns.en: Well, I was telling you how I used Starlink when I was in Utah. I was in the mountains of Utah, there was no cell phone service anywhere near, and we had full YouTube, we had text messages, FaceTime, everything, phone calls. It was nuts.
turns.zh: 嗯，我跟你讲过我在犹他州用星链的事。我在犹他州的山区，附近完全没有手机信号，但我们能看 YouTube、发短信、用 FaceTime、打电话，什么都能做。太疯狂了。

turns.en: Yeah, and it was this big as that cigar box. Yeah, it's crazy. It's so light. When I brought it out there, like that's it? I just plug it in, and the guys I was in camp with, they were like, this is crazy. Yeah, the whole camp was sharing it, like 10 people using the Wi-Fi signal. Right, it's nuts.
turns.zh: 是啊，而且它只有那个雪茄盒那么大。太疯狂了。它非常轻。我把它带到那里时，他们问：就这个？我插上电，和我一起露营的家伙们都说，这太疯狂了。整个营地都在共享它，大概 10 个人用 Wi-Fi 信号。没错，太不可思议了。

turns.en: Yep, and then you know that's the beginning. I mean, what you're at right now is like what version? This is Starlink Mini, right? So this is like a very small version. How much smaller can it scale down from that?
turns.zh: 是的，而且你知道这只是开始。你现在用的是哪个版本？这是星链 Mini 对吧？所以这是一个非常小的版本。它能缩小到什么程度？

turns.en: Well, there's a certain area that you need. The bigger the area, the more you can like bandwidth, because you're trying to catch these photons essentially. So you can think of the area of the antenna: the more area you have, the more photons you can catch. But we have a direct-to-cell capability as well that we're just launching. That will turn on in probably a few months. That'll actually connect directly to a cell phone unmodified. But because the cell phone is a much worse antenna than a dedicated antenna, it'll be about 100 times less bandwidth, but still you'll be able to do text messages, pictures, medium resolution videos, that kind of thing.
turns.zh: 嗯，你需要一定的面积。面积越大，带宽就越大，因为你本质上是在捕捉光子。所以你可以把天线面积想象成：面积越大，能捕捉的光子就越多。但我们也有直接到手机的能力，刚刚开始发射。大概几个月后就会启用。它将能直接连接到未经修改的手机。但由于手机天线比专用天线差得多，带宽会低大约 100 倍，但你仍然可以发短信、图片、中等分辨率视频之类的。

turns.en: One of the cool things about the new phone, the new iPhone 16, I got it and I was in the mountains last month and I was text messaging with satellites. Yeah, iMessages, right? And receiving them, but just text. Yeah, just text. But still pretty impressive.
turns.zh: 新手机 iPhone 16 的一个很酷的功能是，我上个月在山区时，用卫星发短信。是的，iMessage，对吧？还能接收，但只是文字。嗯，只是文字。但还是很令人印象深刻。

turns.en: Yeah, yeah. I mean, what are we going to be looking at 100 years from now?
turns.zh: 是啊。我的意思是，100 年后我们会看到什么？

turns.en: 100 years from now, I hope civilization's around. Yeah, that'll be a win.
turns.zh: 100 年后，我希望文明还在。是的，那就算赢了。

turns.en: Yeah, yeah. What are the chances that we blow this whole thing up? 50%? It's hard to say. I mean, I don't think civilization will be totally destroyed unless there's some really massive global thermonuclear war. But Stephen Hawking would say that there's at least a 1% chance of total annihilation every century. That was his rough estimate. But there's a much bigger chance of civilization being less capable than it is today. Because if you look at various civilizations throughout history, whether it's the ancient Sumerians, the Egyptians, the Romans, they have a life cycle: they reached a peak and then started subsiding. So I think a bigger question is whether our technology level will be better or worse than it is today in 100 years. I think it's probably going to be better, but any estimates are going to have so many dependencies. An estimate, I think, is not sure it has any meaning, because there are so many things that can happen in 100 years.
turns.zh: 是啊。我们搞砸这一切的概率有多大？50%？很难说。我的意思是，我不认为文明会被完全摧毁，除非发生某种大规模全球热核战争。但斯蒂芬·霍金会说，每个世纪至少有 1% 的彻底毁灭概率。那是他的粗略估计。但文明能力不如今天的可能性要大得多。因为如果你看看历史上的各种文明，无论是古代苏美尔人、埃及人还是罗马人，它们都有生命周期：达到顶峰然后开始衰落。所以我认为更大的问题是，100 年后我们的技术水平会比今天更好还是更差。我认为可能会更好，但任何估计都有太多依赖因素。我认为一个估计不一定有意义，因为 100 年里可能发生太多事情。

turns.en: Well, the logical hope is always that people pay attention to history and recognize the patterns of how civilizations have collapsed, and recognize what's going wrong in the current society, and say we have to do our best to mitigate this. We've seen this happen before, let's course correct and manage what we've got here now, because it's pretty extraordinary.
turns.zh: 嗯，合乎逻辑的希望总是人们关注历史，认识到文明崩溃的模式，并意识到当前社会出了什么问题，然后说我们必须尽力缓解。我们以前见过这种情况，让我们纠正方向，管理好我们现在拥有的，因为它非常非凡。

turns.en: Yes, this is what we're hoping for with this election. This is what we're hoping for with the future: that people can see we are on a bad path and something can be done right now, and it might be the only moment in history where this is possible. Because if they do lock the country down and make it so that voting is kind of you're voting for primaries and the people that they put in the primaries, they're controlling that in the first place, you don't really have democracy anymore. You don't really have choice. Exactly. You don't really have freedom. That's right.
turns.zh: 是的，这就是我们对这次选举的希望。这就是我们对未来的希望：人们可以看到我们走在一条糟糕的道路上，现在可以做一些事情，这可能是历史上唯一可能的时刻。因为如果他们真的封锁国家，让投票变成你是在为初选投票，而他们放在初选里的人从一开始就受他们控制，那么你就不再真正拥有民主了。你不再真正有选择。没错。你不再真正有自由。对。

turns.en: Yeah, I think freedom is fundamentally at stake in the election tomorrow, and we'll know. I think we'll know by the end of day tomorrow. I don't think it's going to take days after the election. I think we'll know tomorrow. Are you optimistic?
turns.zh: 是的，我认为自由在明天的选举中岌岌可危，我们会知道的。我认为明天结束前就会知道。我认为不会等到选举后几天。我认为明天就会知道。你乐观吗？

turns.en: I am currently optimistic, but the biggest factor here is that men need to vote. That is the biggest issue. I don't know what the reason is, but men just vote at a much lower rate than women. I think it's like 9%? Someone just told me that today. It's a big difference. And I'm just saying, this is a message to the men out there: vote like your life depends on it, because I think it does. Vote tomorrow like your life depends on it. Nothing is more important.
turns.zh: 我目前是乐观的，但这里最大的因素是男性需要投票。这是最大的问题。我不知道原因是什么，但男性的投票率远低于女性。我想大概是 9%？今天有人刚告诉我。这是一个很大的差距。我只想说，这是给所有男性的信息：像你的生命取决于此一样去投票，因为我认为确实如此。明天去投票，就像你的生命取决于此一样。没有什么比这更重要了。

turns.en: I agree. Yeah, listen, man, thank you for being here. I know you're busy, so I really appreciate your time. And again, I thank you so much for buying Twitter, because I really do believe that you've changed the course of history. I really do think you've created a pathway where people can actually express themselves and exchange information that really didn't exist before, and I think it was dangerous. It is dangerous.
turns.zh: 我同意。是的，听着，伙计，谢谢你来到这里。我知道你很忙，所以我真的很感激你的时间。再次，我非常感谢你买下 Twitter，因为我真的相信你改变了历史的进程。我真的认为你创造了一条人们可以真正表达自己和交换信息的途径，这在以前并不存在，而且我认为它曾经很危险。它确实很危险。

turns.en: Hopefully I live long enough to see my kids grow up and people on Mars. That's all I'm asking for here. I don't think that's too much to happen.
turns.zh: 希望我能活得足够长，看到我的孩子长大，看到人类登上火星。这就是我在这里所要求的。我不认为这太过分。

turns.en: Thank you very much. Appreciate it. Thank you. Pleasure. All right, bye.
turns.zh: 非常感谢。很感激。谢谢。荣幸。好的，再见。

consensus.en: Freedom of speech is the bedrock of democracy; without it, democracy is an illusion.
consensus.zh: 言论自由是民主的基石；没有它，民主就是幻觉。

consensus.en: The mainstream media is a mouthpiece for the Democratic Party, not balanced.
consensus.zh: 主流媒体是民主党的传声筒，并不平衡。

consensus.en: Community Notes on X is the best counter to misinformation through transparency.
consensus.zh: X上的社区笔记通过透明度成为对抗错误信息的最佳方式。

consensus.en: Illegal immigration into swing states threatens the integrity of U.S. elections.
consensus.zh: 非法移民涌入摇摆州威胁美国选举的公正性。

consensus.en: The government's interference in social media, like the Twitter Files, is unconstitutional.
consensus.zh: 政府对社交媒体的干预，如Twitter文件，是违宪的。

contrarian.en: Playing video games can make you a better surgeon, reducing errors by 32%.
contrarian.zh: 玩电子游戏可以让你成为更好的外科医生，减少32%的错误。

contrarian.en: Eating red meat has negligible impact on global warming; it's irrelevant.
contrarian.zh: 吃红肉对全球变暖的影响微乎其微，无关紧要。

contrarian.en: Creatine improves mental performance when sleep-deprived, acting as a nootropic.
contrarian.zh: 肌酸在睡眠不足时改善心智表现，起到益智药的作用。

contrarian.en: The Second Amendment protects the First Amendment by deterring tyranny.
contrarian.zh: 第二修正案通过威慑暴政来保护第一修正案。

contrarian.en: If a drug can make you a better person, it should be legal; if not, it shouldn't.
contrarian.zh: 如果一种药物能让你成为更好的人，它就应该合法；否则就不应该。

contrarian.en: The Democratic Party is importing illegals to turn swing states blue permanently.
contrarian.zh: 民主党正在输入非法移民以永久地将摇摆州变蓝。
